@wesm When you say "distributed alongside", do you mean "comes with", as in it gets absorbed by those projects and ceases to be separate? In that case, I would agree with you.

If you mean "distributed alongside" as in "a GNU/Linux distribution can ship this package and ship IPython and they work together", I don't believe a re-licensing is needed.

Or do you mean something different?

I'm not arguing that you *shouldn't* relicense this package (it is your right as the copyright holders of course!) I want to make sure everyone is clear on what the GPL requires, because there is a large amount of misinformation out there about it and many people are afraid of it due to this.

I personally think the GPL is a great license for users *and for developers* because it guarantees that a developer's efforts cannot be leeched by someone else who wants to proprietize the work. For example, if I spend 10 years making librbarlow, I don't want Closed Source, Inc. to take it and incorporate it into some software they are selling that doesn't respect their users' freedom. If I choose GPL, that would be disallowed by the license and I personally find that very attractive as a developer. Of course, this does not appeal to everyone but I think it's a point worth considering for yourselves.

From a user's perspective it's not as important because any software licensed to them that guarantees their four freedoms is fine (like the BSD or MIT).

Either way, I'm happy that you are staying with a free license!

@Kuniwak basically a good game plan, I'm not sure though if how this impacts performance if people start loading megabyte-sized vim files into the linter (since you're basically loading everything into memory).
Thinking about it now, I consider that quite unlikely actually :laughing:, so maybe we should wait till that happens.

Then again, from what I know about python, stdin is just a specialized file object, so if we pass the opened file in instead of just the path we could substitute that with the stdin object without any other part of the system noticing. This would at least give the possibility to keep the linter memory efficient, without yielding too much overhead compared to just using raw text.

It's your software though, so if you can't be bothered don't let my inner system architect's nagging stop you; storing the file content in context is most likely a perfectly valid solution :+1:

Hi. Could you provide an example of some failing code (changing/omitting organization/connection information and IDs)?

Looking at the docs it appears you will probably want to send in the list of IDs as a comma separated string. I mention this just in case you are attempting to send a list through. The list could end up getting mangled in the later code such that only the last one is being processed. Or, it could be passing everything in a weird way and only the last one is getting processed on the Zendesk service side.

If you have a list, try sending a string instead with something like:

```
ids = ['1', '2', '3']
zd.organizations_destroy_many(ids=','.join(ids))
```

or

```
ids = [1, 2, 3]
zd.organizations_destroy_many(ids=','.join(repr(i) for i in ids))
```

things they don't understand.  It would be very easy for someone who was
careless or didn't understand the algorithm to mess up the layer if they
could easily edit anything. My general attitude is that if you are
sufficiently unfamiliar with C++ that you can't figure out how to modify
this then you probably shouldn't. So I'm not going to make it super easy to
do this.

entire loss_mmod layer by assigning to it with operator =. That wouldn't
involve a const_cast.

> One note: In order to get the whole response back, I needed to remove the not on line 80 of the example. Does this make sense to you?

I do not think so. Also, what do you mean by whole response?

That condition just stops sending GETBULK query for *next* OIDs once the agent tells as that there are no more *next* OIDs to report.

Here is some non-debugged code which should give you an idea on the concurrent/async approach. Essentially, you send many packets into socket at once, then wait for *any* response to arrive whenever they happen to arrive. The fact that you do not wait for each of your agent, but wait for all of them at once - this is what saves time if you have many/slow agents.

> I'd be willing to attempt writing an asyncio wrapper for the low-level API. Do you have any suggestions for how best to go about it?

As for asyncio wrapper, you essentially introduce the [asyncio.Future](https://github.com/etingof/pysnmp/blob/master/pysnmp/hlapi/asyncio/cmdgen.py#L149) object to maintain the state of the pending request + add some coroutine/generator wrapper.

Does it make sense?

```
from pysnmp.carrier.asyncore.dispatch import AsyncoreDispatcher
from pysnmp.carrier.asyncore.dgram import udp
from pyasn1.codec.ber import encoder, decoder
from pysnmp.proto.api import v2c
from time import time


class SnmpManager(object):

    def __init__(self):
        self._pending_reqs = {}
        self._results = {}

        self.transportDispatcher = AsyncoreDispatcher()

        self.transportDispatcher.registerRecvCbFun(self.cbRecvFun)
        self.transportDispatcher.registerTimerCbFun(self.cbTimerFun)

        self.transportDispatcher.registerTransport(
            udp.domainName, udp.UdpSocketTransport().openClientMode()
        )

    def cbTimerFun(self, timeNow):
        for reqId, req in self._pending_reqs.items():
            startedAt = self._pending_reqs['startedAt']
            del self._pending_reqs[reqId]
            if timeNow - startedAt > 3:
                # TODO: retry timed out request before bailing out
                raise Exception("Request %s timed out" % reqId)


    # noinspection PyUnusedLocal
    def cbRecvFun(self, transportDispatcher,
                  transportDomain, transportAddress,
                  wholeMsg):
        stop = False

        while wholeMsg:
            rspMsg, wholeMsg = decoder.decode(wholeMsg, asn1Spec=v2c.Message())

            rspPdu = v2c.apiMessage.getPDU(rspMsg)

            reqId = v2c.apiBulkPDU.getRequestID(rspPdu)

            try:
                dst = self._pending_reqs[reqId]['dst']
                startedAt = self._pending_reqs[reqId]['startedAt']
                match_oid = self._pending_reqs[reqId]['matchOid']
                trim_len = self._pending_reqs[reqId]['trimLen']
                headVars = self._pending_reqs[reqId]['headVars']
                reqMsg =  self._pending_reqs[reqId]['reqMsg']
                reqPdu =  self._pending_reqs[reqId]['reqPdu']
                del self._pending_reqs[reqId]

            except KeyError:
                print('Late response %d ignored' % reqId)
                transportDispatcher.jobFinished(1)
                continue

            # Match response to request
            if v2c.apiBulkPDU.getRequestID(reqPdu) == v2c.apiBulkPDU.getRequestID(rspPdu):
                # Format var-binds table
                varBindTable = v2c.apiBulkPDU.getVarBindTable(reqPdu, rspPdu)

                # Check for SNMP errors reported
                errorStatus = v2c.apiBulkPDU.getErrorStatus(rspPdu)
                if errorStatus and errorStatus != 2:
                    errorIndex = v2c.apiBulkPDU.getErrorIndex(rspPdu)
                    print('%s at %s' % (errorStatus.prettyPrint(),
                                        errorIndex and varBindTable[int(errorIndex) - 1] or '?'))
                    transportDispatcher.jobFinished(1)
                    break

                result = self._results[dst] = {}

                # Report SNMP table
                for tableRow in varBindTable:
                    for name, val in tableRow:
                        # print(str(name),int(val))
                        oid = str(name)
                        if oid.startswith(match_oid):
                            result[oid[trim_len:]] = int(val)
                        else:
                            stop = True
                            # print(name.prettyPrint())
                            break
                    if stop:
                        break

                # Stop on EOM
                for oid, val in varBindTable[-1]:
                    if isinstance(val, v2c.Null):
                        stop = True
                        break

                if stop:
                    transportDispatcher.jobFinished(1)
                    break

                # Generate request for next row
                v2c.apiBulkPDU.setVarBinds(
                    reqPdu, [(x, v2c.null) for x, y in varBindTable[-1]]
                )
                v2c.apiBulkPDU.setRequestID(reqPdu, v2c.getNextRequestID())
                transportDispatcher.sendMessage(
                    encoder.encode(reqMsg), transportDomain, transportAddress
                )
                if time() - startedAt[0] > 3:
                    raise Exception('Request timed out')

                startedAt[0] = time()

        return wholeMsg


    def getbulktrimmed(self, base_oid, dst, comm):
        match_oid = base_oid.strip('.') + '.'
        trim_len = len(match_oid)
        # SNMP table header
        headVars = [v2c.ObjectIdentifier(base_oid)]

        # Build PDU
        reqPdu = v2c.GetBulkRequestPDU()
        v2c.apiBulkPDU.setDefaults(reqPdu)
        v2c.apiBulkPDU.setNonRepeaters(reqPdu, 0)
        v2c.apiBulkPDU.setMaxRepetitions(reqPdu, 5000)
        v2c.apiBulkPDU.setVarBinds(reqPdu, [(x, v2c.null) for x in headVars])

        # Build message
        reqMsg = v2c.Message()
        v2c.apiMessage.setDefaults(reqMsg)
        v2c.apiMessage.setCommunity(reqMsg, comm)
        v2c.apiMessage.setPDU(reqMsg, reqPdu)

        reqId = v2c.apiBulkPDU.getRequestID(reqPdu)

        self._pending_reqs[reqId] = {
            'dst': dst,
            'startedAt': [time()],
            'matchOid': match_oid,
            'tripLen': trim_len,
            'headVars': headVars,
            'reqMsg': reqMsg,
            'reqPdu': reqPdu
        }

        self.transportDispatcher.sendMessage(
            encoder.encode(reqMsg), udp.domainName, dst,
           # Alternatively to maintaining `self._pendingReqs` we could just pass our state
           # to this function (as kw args), then get them back in `cbFun`. But retrying
           # timed out request would be difficult then.
        )
        self.transportDispatcher.jobStarted(1)


    def run(self):
        # this will complete as no more running jobs remain
        self._results.clear()
        self.transportDispatcher.runDispatcher()
        return self._results

    def close(self):
        self.transportDispatcher.closeDispatcher()


if __name__ == '__main__':

            base_oid, (switch, port), comm
        )

    results = snmpManager.run()
    print(results)
    snmpManager.close()

Hey Konrad, so the distance between the last two points in your example is 9.99999993923e-09     
                                                                                          
Vertices will be merged if they are closer* than trimesh.constants.tol.merge, which is set by default to 1e-8 (which is the same default value Solidworks uses).                   
                                                                                          
9.99999993923e-09 is within floating point weirdness of tol.merge, so I think what you want to do here is just set a larger tol.merge. I just committed a change and did a release (get with pip install trimesh --upgrade) which allows you to monkey patch tol.merge:

```
trimesh.constants.tol.merge = 1e-7                                                        
```

I may add an additional kwarg back to merge_vertices to override the default value in the future, for clarity. Also note that in your example the one face is degenerate or zero area and will be removed by process(). If you just want the points merged you can do: 
```                                                                  
unique, inverse = trimesh.grouping.unique_rows(v)
v_unique = v[unique] 
```                                                                                      

Here's your original code block with monkeypatching:
```
import numpy as np
import trimesh
v = np.array([[ 1. , 1.60994057, 2.07798185],
              [ 1. , 0.60994057, 2.07798184],
              [ 1. , 0.60994057, 2.07798185]])
f = np.array([[0,1,2]])

trimesh.constants.tol.merge = 1e-7

# note that process will merge vertices and remove degenerate faces                       
mesh = trimesh.Trimesh(vertices=v, faces=f, process=True)
print mesh.vertices
```

*closer: any single value doesn't exceed tol.merge, which means the points can have norms
up to 1.73*tol.merge (aka 3**.5)

so I've pushed a few more commits in the branch [requests/52](https://github.com/guyzmo/git-repo/tree/request/52)… I tried pushing new commits to this PR but it looks like it's not allowed by github.

About the cassettes, it's quite simple actually. In [`conftests.py` I've configured the record mode to happen once](https://github.com/guyzmo/git-repo/blob/request/52/tests/conftest.py#L8). So it means that if you delete the cassette matching the test like [`test_gitlab_01_create__already_exists`](https://github.com/guyzmo/git-repo/blob/request/52/tests/integration/cassettes/test_gitlab_test_01_create__already_exists.json) it will start a new record of the HTTP exchanges happening with the code.

That means that you need to make sure that the test will behave as expected, i.e. for that last one, you need to have an existing repository with name `git-repo` in your own namespace.

Also, because the "default" namespace isn't guessed at testing time (I used to use your current's user username per default, but I figured it's actually wrong), it's using the environment variable `GITREPO_NAMESPACE` as your default namespace. So if you start the tests like:

``` shell
% rm /tests/integration/cassettes/test_gitlab_test_01_create__already_exists.json
% GITREPO_NAMESPACE=foobar bin/py.test
```

It will expect that your gitlab login username is `foobar`, and that there is a `foobar/git-repo` project existing in your project.

Then, writing a new test is easy, I've built a bunch of [helper methods](https://github.com/guyzmo/git-repo/blob/request/52/tests/helpers.py#L448-L784) that mirrors the API of the services, which takes care of all the cassette handling and the git repository mocking up. So to write a new test you need to use those methods and create [a new usage scenario](https://github.com/guyzmo/git-repo/blob/request/52/tests/integration/test_gitlab.py#L50-L52):

``` python
    def test_01_create_group__new(self):
        self.action_create(namespace='git-repo-test', repository='foobar')
```

I don't know what your code looks like, or what problem you are trying to solve, so it is impossible to give an exact answer.

In general though, even if your math is perfect, in the end we are linearizing a nonlinear system, and errors will accrue. In practice you need to do a few things:

1) P = 0.5 P + 0.5P^T. This ensures the matrix is symmetric.

2) P = P + eI, where e is some very small number, and I is the identity matrix. This ensures you don't underflow. there is some content in wikipedia on the condition number of a matrix: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Condition_number

I did some digging and tested some TorMySQL and Torpeewee boilerplate code and both work fine with current Tornado versions and native coroutines.

Further things I've found (and written down so I can make sense of it):

- `concurrent.futures.Future` does _not_ implement `__await__` so they are not compatible with `await` - `tornado.concurrent.Future` does however (and that's what TorMySQL uses a lot), so that's part of why it's working out-of-the-box (also Tornado flags the generator functions as native coroutines in newer Python versions to make them compatible with `async/await`
- Using an `asyncio` loop requires wrapping all `await` calls with `tornado.platform.asyncio.to_asyncio_future` (of course), as that's replacing `concurrent.futures.Future` for newer Python versions (and which _does_ implement `__await__`).

So as for Python 3.5 no changes to the project(s) are needed and for Tornado 5 it might require "wrapping" every legacy Future into an asyncio-style future (but the decorator might take care of that). 

Still leaves the 28x speed increase on the table though (but Tornado 5 might remedy that).

(Feel free to close the issue then).

Ok, so the problem is that Windows is telling us that two virtual key codes are mapped to the same scan code. This also happens with `d` and `volume mute`, for example, but the bug is not triggered out of luck (the correct one appears first in the list).

When you say you want `print screen`,  there's a function that maps it to a scan code. But that Windows wants to send events is a virtual key code, so we have to convert. But because of the previous ambiguity, this scan code may end up triggering the wrong key.

A solution is to map it first to a virtual key code, *then* to a scan code. Even if the scan code is wrong, what matters for sending events is the virtual key code, so it should be ok (with exception of some enterprise software that forwards events, but that's another matter).

So that's what I did. Now when the Windows backend is mapping a name, it first tries to map to a virtual key code, and only goes for scan codes if none is found. The downside is that this is less portable between Windows and Linux, and the whole thing is a bit hacky.

But it should be working now.

Pip updates are triggered by the developer. If you are curious about the process:

- Bumping the version in `setup.py` and running `python setup.py sdist upload` is sufficient to package and upload to PyPI. However for this project we also need to...
- Run tests to make sure nothing is too broken or missing too much coverage.
- Decide the new version based on [Semantic Versioning](http://semver.org/).
- Update `CHANGES.md` listing what has changed.
- Update API docs in `README.md`.
- Convert docs into RST text format for PyPI.
- Add a Git tag with the new version number.
- Push changes to GitHub.
- Create a new Github release listing the changes and including the source code compressed files.

Thankfully this was all automated a while ago in `make_release.py`.

My initial suggestion of using a timed read was flawed, unfortunately; took me a while to remember some of my systems programming. This will end up giving us a busy-wait situation because the process is going to wake up each time the read times out.

It seems that urllib3's handling of chunked streams is simply not designed to handle this type of use case; in fact, it seems actively hostile to it. For instance, setting chunk_size to None as mentioned earlier causes it to ignore the last 2 bytes of whatever it has determined to be the appropriate length to read:
```python
        if amt is None:
            chunk = self._fp._safe_read(self.chunk_left)
            returned_chunk = chunk
            self._fp._safe_read(2)  # Toss the CRLF at the end of the chunk.
            self.chunk_left = None
```

Ultimately, the problem seems to be that for whatever reason, either urllib3, http.client, or both do not support short reads properly unless it hits EOF when reading; using a chunk size of 1024 should really not cause a problem like this. It's going to take some more digging to figure out why, but `recv` and `recvfrom` are supposed to do short reads and not block unless you have the `MSG_WAITALL` flag set.

First of all, as a workaround, on v0.8, it should be possible to change line 898 of node.py to:
```python
                except (OSError, dns.resolver.NoNameservers, OpenSSL.SSL.Error) as exc:
```

Secondly, the issue is twofold: (a) this error should not prevent reconnection and (b) this specific error should not happen. (a) is addressed with the workaround above.

(b) is more tricky. This is a bug in aioopenssl. According to a [stackoverflow answer](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2997218/why-am-i-getting-error1409f07fssl-routinesssl3-write-pending-bad-write-retr):

> For example, when SSL_write(ssl, ptr, size) with ptr = 0xABCDEFGH, size = 4096 fails with SSL_ERROR_WANT_READ or SSL_ERROR_WANT_WRITE, when retrying the SSL_write call, the parameters ptr and size should be same. It is not equivalent if ptr is another pointer pointing to a copy of the same contents as in the original call. 

This is not enforced in aioopenssl, and this also shows why it happens more often with more traffic: the more traffic, the more likely it is that after an incomplete write (for example, due to a laggy TCP connection caused by an unstable WiFi link), more data is appended to the buffer, changing size and/or ptr.

I will look into solutions for that.

I forgot which version it was, but somewhere between 7.1 and now, I introduced connection pooling, which is implemented in the module `telepot.api`. #87 happened right after connection pooling was introduced. Version 7.1 (the one you were using) did not have connection pooling, meaning every time it contacts Telegram servers, a brand new HTTP connection is used.

So, it seems:

- Connection pooling: need `retries`
- Individual connections: no `retries` needed

Why the difference? I have no idea. I can't think of any changes after 7.1 which may cause such a problem. If I really want to know, I would look more deeply into urllib3. But I have no such motivation.

If you want as much stability as before, you may try to override the function `_which_pool()` to make it use an individual connection every time:

```python
def always_use_new(req, **user_kw):
    return None

telepot.api._which_pool = always_use_new
```

I haven't tried this code, but it should not be far off. The point is, you can use the function `_which_pool()` to dictate which pool to use or to use a new connection.

Another way to achieve more stability is to catch the exception yourself and retry. I understand that's troublesome and is basically shifting the responsibility from the library to the user. That's why I don't expect you to do that, although it *is* one way out.

Good luck.

Hi, I checked the benchmarks and agree  the modifications to keep addresses sorted increase CPU time. I investigated where the additional CPU time comes from, and found that:

1. The new iterators in `IPaddrSet` are slower, as they are not built-in.
2. `LinkedSet` (and the old `IPaddrSet`) do not keep field `raw` up-to-date when new instances are created from iterators (e.g., in code like `ips_to_remove = set1 - set2`) [A]. One reason for this is that  `__init__` in `LinkedSet` does not call `add`. Given we are using `raw` to keep track of the order or addresses, we need to keep it up to date, which takes extra cycles. (This is why we have `__init__` in `IPaddrSet`.)

To try and make it easy for you to check, I benchmarked only chancing `raw` to `OrderedDict` and found that it has equivalent performance to plain `dict`. I have made a [separate pull request][1] in case you want to test it on your side (I can rebase this pull request on top of the other one if you'd prefer that). Numbers:

 [1]: https://github.com/svinota/pyroute2/pull/303

```
root@desktop:~/pyroute2# for i in $(seq 10) ; do \time -o master.times --append -f "%U %S %E" python benchmark/ipdb.py ; done
root@desktop:~/pyroute2# git checkout linkedset-ordereddict
root@desktop:~/pyroute2# for i in $(seq 10) ; do \time -o ordereddict.times --append -f "%U %S %E" python benchmark/ipdb.py ; done
root@desktop:~/pyroute2# cut -d " " -f 1 master.times  | ../bin/stats --mean
3.07300
root@desktop:~/pyroute2# cut -d " " -f 1 ordereddict.times  | ../bin/stats --mean                                                                                        
3.06700
```


[A] This actually confused me a bit when first looking at the code; I guess it is works fine because when pyroute2 creates new `IPaddrSet`s from set differences, the `raw` dictionary is no longer needed. But this understanding might be wrong.

Recording my design decision here for reference:

Single Responsibility Principle
---------------------------------------
One of the background refactoring goals is to move toward observation of the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP), in which each class would be conceptually responsible for one thing. Session is a huge violation currently.

The Real World
--------------------
However, given the state of the project, separating concerns can be a real pain. This is because the program has been previously developed in violation of the SRP and Separation of Concerns.

As you said, a proper refactor would be a comparatively huge effort to the proposed change. It'd be frustrating and anti-pragmatic to block this feature since it's currently so easy to implement. 

Future
---------
If you're trying to expand the procmon's capabilities, and if you need to give it various options, consider sending options via `procmon_options`, if you haven't already. Example from [here](http://www.securepla.net/download/ftp.txt):

```
target.procmon_options =  {
	"proc_name" : "smallftpd.exe",
	"stop_commands" : ['wmic process where (name="smallftpd.exe") delete"'],
	"start_commands" : ['C:\\Documents and Settings\\Administrator\\Desktop\\smallftpd-1.0.3-fix\\smallftpd.exe'],
}
```

This allows the configuration to stay on the fuzzing host. It is transmitted to the procmon host over RPC.

No.  The whole point of `Arc` is that it treats all the points as being collapsed onto the unit sphere and only considers the separations along great circles of that sphere.

The two things that are usually done when using photometric redshifts are
1. Define some modest number of photo-z bins and compute the auto- and cross-correlations among the various bins.  Especially because photo-zs are generally not super accurate, this lets you just calibrate the overall N(z) within each bin, which is generally much more reliable than trusting each individual photo-z estimate.
2. Give all your objects a full 3D position (ra, dec, r) and use one of the metrics that use physical distances, rather than angular separation.  In TreeCorr, the options are currently `Rlens`, `Rperp` and `Euclidean`.  The former two allow for the `min_rpar` and `max_rpar` parameters.  The third does not.

If neither of these suffice for your science case, you could slice your catalog into many shells in redshift and use Arc for the cross-correlations between shells that fit your rpar criterion.  TreeCorr would still be fairly efficient doing things that way, but there is currently no option for TreeCorr to do that for you.

Here is a small snippet of code that shows how nested resource serializers could be defined and proofs that it works:

```python
from pprint import pprint

from graceful.serializers import BaseSerializer
from graceful.fields import StringField


class Inner(BaseSerializer):
    fooX = StringField("Foo field", source='foo')
    barX = StringField("Bar field", source='bar')


class Outer(BaseSerializer):
    innerX = Inner(details="inner object", source='inner')
    nameX = StringField("Name field", source='name')


def main():
    outer = Outer()

    internal = {
        "inner": {
            "foo": "something",
            "bar": "something else",
        },
        "name": "my outer"
    }

    print("\n ====== internal ======")
    pprint(internal)

    print("\n === representation ===")
    pprint(outer.to_representation(internal))

    print("\n ====== internal ======")
    pprint(outer.from_representation(outer.to_representation(internal)))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

And example output:

```console
$ python nested.py 

 ====== internal ======
{'inner': {'bar': 'something else', 'foo': 'something'}, 'name': 'my outer'}

 === representation ===
{'innerX': {'barX': 'something else', 'fooX': 'something'}, 'nameX': 'my outer'}

 ====== internal ======
{'inner': {'bar': 'something else', 'foo': 'something'}, 'name': 'my outer'}
```

But the problem is in how we will handle "describe()" methods in serializers. If they are fields now we should make them described the same way  as fields so with "details", "type", "label", "spec", "read_only" attributes. Right now we put all definition to "fields" dictionary of resource metadata dict but we cannot handle this that way anymore. 

I think we could utilise serializer class docstring as a field details and completely change the overall layout of description output. Instead of having single `fields` dict in the resource metadata we could have something more nested:

```
{
    ...,
    'representation': {  # => result of serializer.describe()
         'details': ... # => serializers docstring
         'label': ...    # => optional label for consistency
         'spec': ...    # => spec, here developer can add link for docs in case of nested serializers
         'type': 'representation', # => optionally 'object'/'dict' to inform it is another dict
         'fields': {  # => content of former 'fields' dictionary
             { # example of single field like str, int, float etc.
                  'details': ...
                  'label': ...
                  'spec': ...
                  'type': ....
                  # => in case of simple fields there is no 'fields' key
              },
              { # example of field coming from different serializer
                  'details': ...
                  'label': ...
                  'spec': ...
                  'type': 'representation'
                  'fields': {...}  # => fields that are serializers will have the same description
              },
         },
    },
}
```
Alternative way of handling nested descriptions is utilisation of `spec` field attribute that will now
come really handy. We could assume that resource description structure stays as it is (flat `fields` dict) and whenever we include serializer as a field the user should supply `spec` with the link of nested resource serialisation. Because resources are self-descriptive (full metadata on OPTIONS request) this can be simply a link to URI of separate nested resource endpoint.

Pros:
* in when user would like to create mutually nested resources (it is possible) we will not end up with infinite description loop
* it makes possible auto documenters simpler beach they will not need to deal with nested fields descriptions
* it does not require backwards incompatible changes

Cons:
* it will be harder to describe nested resources that do not have their own query endpoints

For the problem with describing nested resources that do not have their now API there is a simple solution. One could create virtual endpoint that serves only documentation purpose. It would accept only OPTIONS requests to serve specification but would not deliver any of other manipulation methods (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE).

Also the main reason for having nested resource capabilities is a use case when serializer that is nested is actually reused in some other existing resource endpoint.

@Lasconik Yes, you see the complexity involved.

The details of relationships are abstracted out into the pptx.opc sub-package; so things like looking up an image part be the r:Id used in the pic element is accomplished with calls like:

```python
image_part = shape.part.related_parts[rId]
```

Also in the `opc` subpackage is the `OpcPackage` class, which represents all the parts and all their relationships. Basically it's the .pptx file once opened.

A reference to the package can be gotten from a part:

```python
package = shape.part.package
```

And all the relationships in the package can be accessed from there:

```python
for rel in package.iter_rels():
    if rel.target is the image file:
        usage count += 1
```

So I'd be inclined to have it just traverse all the rels when needed rather than try to maintain a synchronized dictionary or something that you have to remember to update.

Not sure if related, but I noticed that there are some weird issues with sc2.bot_ai.find_placement(). I noticed this when trying to make a custom warp_in function, because I couldn't get it to warp in further out from the targetted pylon, regardless of what I changed max_distance to, I kept getting placement errors.

I solved this by just warping in at random positions, so I didn't have to use find_placement() or query_building_placement() at all, and I sent the action manually using self._client.actions() to surpress the error logging. As a side-note, I kind of wish you could disable error-logging with an extra argument to sc2.bot_ai.do() so I could use that directly instead.

Here's my custom warp-in solution:

```
    async def warp_in(self, unit, location, warpgate):
        if isinstance(location, sc2.unit.Unit):
            location = location.position.to2
        elif location is not None:
            location = location.to2

        x = random.randrange(-8,8)
        y = random.randrange(-8,8)

        placement = sc2.position.Point2((location.x+x,location.y+y))

        action = warpgate.warp_in(unit, placement)
        error = await self._client.actions(action, game_data=self._game_data)

        if not error:
            cost = self._game_data.calculate_ability_cost(action.ability)
            self.minerals -= cost.minerals
            self.vespene -= cost.vespene
            return None
        else:
            return error
```

It looks like zsh is being smarter than bash and giving COMP_POINT in unicode chars instead of bytes. When we incorrectly interpret that number, we end up slicing the input halfway through a character, which we then fail to decode. Even if that didn't crash, the results would likely be undesirable.

I used bashcompinit as described [here](https://github.com/kislyuk/argcomplete/issues/10#issuecomment-19369876) to reproduce this. I'm assuming bashcompinit is supposed to emulate bash completion, in which case I'd say this should be considered a bug on their end.

On the other hand, [the documentation for COMP_POINT](https://www.gnu.org/software/bash/manual/html_node/Bash-Variables.html#Bash-Variables) explicitly says "If the current cursor position is at the end of the current command, the value of this variable is equal to ${#COMP_LINE}." So bashcompinit is adhering to the spec and bash is not.

Though safe_reg_next is set near the top of the function (line 330), it is later passed to the register_form (line 355). At the end of the POST processing, this must then be retrieved from register_form.reg_next.data.

I think your issue is caused by the if statement:
    if 'reg_next' in request.args:
which will be false if the POST url no longer contains the 'reg_next' query param.

The fix is:
    if register_form.reg_next.data

I think this syntax for get_history call would be nice ```scripthash.get_history( address, from_index, count )```. "from_index" and "count" can be used for pagination, and if the client only wants one transaction (for example, when it is scanning for active accounts during wallet recovery) he can make the process more efficient by specifying that just 1 address should be returned. For more flexibility, Python convention might be adopted that used negative indices as indices from the end of the array/list, though it's not a very useful idea as far as mempool is concerned... Maybe a boolean flag specifying whether mempool transactions should be also returned can be added.

Now, to make this work, as far as I understand, you'd need to store a snapshot of address' history in memory on the first call to get_history for that address (probably in a LRU cache). Because transactions, especially mempool ones, can be returned in any order by the daemon, as far as I understand, and also because a reorg might happen between two paginated calls. In this case, reorg won't be reflected in the results, but at least the client would get a consistent result.

I saw a suggestion to paginate the results based on block height, but I don't think this would work nicely with the "count" parameter - which is important for reducing network load - because several transactions could happen with one address in the same block, and the pagination won't be able to handle this case correctly, and mempool transactions don't have block height anyway.

If you want get_history call to return block hash instead of block height, then there is a great need for a call that returns block header by block hash. Currently, block hash is only usable for some local checks, but it can't be used to get anything out of server (like block time) - this requires block height, which we won't have in this case.

Also, do you think it would be possible/desirable to add "batch" versions of API calls that work on arrays of addresses or transaction hashes? Often, clients needs informations about many addresses or transactions at the same time. Currently, this requires making many API calls, which might be less efficient, both in terms of network overhead and server-side processing. Another server I worked with accepted arrays in both its version of get_history and transaction.get, and it was very convenient. It also simplifies client code, because it's easier to wait for a single response than for a group of them (if you want them all to be completed before moving on).
See: https://www.traviscistatus.com/incidents/77q4ts757mzs

@ilevkivskyi : Any comments on this design? I want to make sure I'm not doing anything that won't work well with static type checkers.

The only wart on it is that you'd need to repeat typing information if you wanted to fully specify `__post_init__()`'s params. Here is a class that takes one of it's fields in `__init__()`, and initialize the other in `__post_init__()` via a config file:
```
@dataclass
class C:
    i: int
    j: int = field(init=False, default=None)
    config_file: InitVar[ConfigFileType]
    def __post_init__(self, config_file: ConfigFileType):
        self.j = config_file.read()

c = C(10, my_config_file)
```
Like `ClassVar`, the `InitVar` field (`config_file` in this case) would not be an instance member. And in this case, not even a class member since it's not initialized.

Later today I'll post a note about how this would work with base classes.

@Alexhans, I investigated this issue and it is hidden deep inside the nose library itself and is not related with nose-timer. This time is spent for nose plugins manager to load proxies calls into cache, so first time when `addSuccess` is called plugin manager creates proxy instance and caches it. At second call proxy just taken from the cache which is faster.

https://github.com/nose-devs/nose/blob/master/nose/proxy.py#L164
```python
self.plugins.addSuccess(self.test)
```

https://github.com/nose-devs/nose/blob/master/nose/plugins/manager.py#L248
```python
    def __getattr__(self, call):
        try:
            return self._proxies[call]
        except KeyError:
            proxy = self.proxyClass(call, self._plugins)
            self._proxies[call] = proxy
        return proxy
```

> This logic should be left for the view its self to handle correctly and perhaps return a 400 response.

This can be accomplished by using [`utils.is_ratelimited`](https://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage.html#is_ratelimited), instead of the decorator. Though, again, I would caution that using a user-supplied header for ratelimiting is probably a bad idea.

`block=True` will cause an exception<sup>1</sup> that ends request processing. `block=False` adds an attribute to the request, `request.limited`, that is either `True` or `False`. This allows views to respond differently to ratelimited-requests. For example, a login form may decide to add a CAPTCHA rather than prevent login entirely, to avoid a DOS vector against a specific IP or user.

<sup>1</sup>: The exception extends Django's `PermissionDenied`, so by default it causes a 403 response. It can also be handled by `RatelimitMiddleware` to use a [custom error view](https://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage.html#middleware).

是的，在一些情境下需要变调，目前有如下4四种情况.

**三声变调**
最普通的变调规则即是在一组两个上声（第三声）的合音节中，须将合音节中领先的第一个音节提升到阳平（第二声）。举例来说，你好（nǐ hǎo）为最普通的中文问候语，nǐ与hǎo原调都是上声，不过合音节第三声的“你”须提升为阳平，即“你好”要念为“ní hǎo”。
**一不变调**
当“一”和“不”夹在词语中间念轻声，例如“看一看”、“好不好”等等。
**“一”的变调**
“一”的原调是第一声。当“一”在表示数目时，以及在词尾出现时，念为原调。例如︰一二三四、第一等等。 当“一”与量词配搭时，如量词的声调是第一声、第二声或第三声，“一”念成第四声，例如“一根”、“一钱”和“一种”。如量词的声调是第四声，“一”念成第二声，例如“一个”。
**“不”的变调**
“不”的原调是第四声。当“不”后面跟着第一声、第二声和第三声的字，或者“不”出现在词末，念为原调。例如︰“不高”、“不祥”、“不好”和“我就不”。 当“不”后面的字是第四声，念为第二声，例如“不要”。

SOCIAL_AUTH_PIPELINE = (	
                'social_auth.backends.pipeline.social.social_auth_user',	
                'social_auth.backends.pipeline.associate.associate_by_email',	
                'social_auth.backends.pipeline.user.get_username',	
                'MYAPP.views.MY_NEW_CREATE_USER',
                'social_auth.backends.pipeline.social.associate_user',
                'social_auth.backends.pipeline.social.load_extra_data',
                'social_auth.backends.pipeline.user.update_user_details',	
)

where MYAPP.views.MY_NEW_CREATE_USER will be a function with a different behavior returning the same data the original create_user function does.

@platedodev Looks like you tried to combine Example 1 & 3, but left out some parts.  Assuming you wanted Example 3, the code should look as follows:

```py
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

# enable debugging
import cgi
import cgitb
import cfscrape
import requests

USER_AGENT = "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/50.0.2661.102 Safari/537.36"

cgitb.enable()
form = cgi.FieldStorage()

print "Content-type: text/html"
print ""
if not form.getvalue('url'):
    print "No url."
else:
    url = form.getvalue('url');
    base_url = "http://" if url.startswith("http:") else "https://" + url.rsplit('/')[2]
    http_headers = {'User-Agent': USER_AGENT}
    tokens = cfscrape.get_cookie_string(url=base_url, user_agent=USER_AGENT)
    http_headers.update({'Cookie': tokens[0], 'Referer': base_url})

    r = requests.get(url, headers=http_headers)
    print r.content  # => "<!DOCTYPE html><html><head>..."
```

Make sure to include the headers for each call after scraping for the cookies.  Otherwise use a session and let `cfscrape` handle the cookies internally.

When you create a calendar item the 'start' and 'end'  fields are required. That's probably the source of your `ValueError` exception.

I sent myself a test email with the subject `Test for #239` and then had success with this test:

```python
from exchangelib import Account, Credentials, ItemAttachment, CalendarItem, UTC_NOW
from exchangelib.items import SEND_TO_ALL_AND_SAVE_COPY

a = Account(email, credentials=Credentials('email', 'password'), autodiscover=True)

vcn_email = a.inbox.get(subject='Test for #239')
vcn_appt = CalendarItem(
    folder=a.calendar, 
    subject=vcn_email.subject, 
    start=UTC_NOW(), 
    end=UTC_NOW() + timedelta(hours=2),
)
vcn_appt.attach(ItemAttachment(name=vcn_email.subject, item=vcn_email))
vcn_appt.save(send_meeting_invitations=SEND_TO_ALL_AND_SAVE_COPY)
```

If the `vcn_email` itself has a file attachment, you'll get a `ErrorRequiredPropertyMissing` unless you pull in [this commit](https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib/commit/2804e013c639c7a13565e7b48dff528412c485d7).

You can combine multiple keywords in one:

```python
my_folder.filter(subject='SubjectName', sender='me@me.com')

Well, yes. My objection would be, that a re-init in some interval is generally not needed in a functional environment. I don't see a responsibility for pyhomematic to take care of such situations, and pyhomematic itself is already capable of performing the re-init manually. At least if you can confirm the method I have mentioned one post earlier works.

What would make sense is to add a service for HASS to easily trigger the init via automation. That way you can set it up however you want. It seems more reasonable to me to have some infrastructure capable of monitoring failures to actively trigger the init than doing periodically within the module.
I'm not sure how that would be done in HASS. One though for a automation trigger would be, that the temperature of a sensor hasn't changed for an hour. The thermostats I have tell their current temperature every few minutes, and within an hour there should be at least a 0.1 de- or increase. If that doesn't happen, you probably have to init again and call the service.

from xmlrpc.client import ServerProxy

# Create the proxy object used to communicate with CCU
p = ServerProxy("http://1.2.3.4:2000")

# Turn off again

It didn't seem possible to create the matrix specified in the original comment (with zero cost for matches/mismatches to N, but constant match/mismatch penalty for the other bases) using `matrix_create`, so I've been doing it from a file like so:

```python
MATRIX_TEMPLATE = \
"""# Auto-generated substitution matrix
       A        C        G        T    N
A  %(m)d   %(mm)d   %(mm)d   %(mm)d    0
C  %(mm)d  %(m)d   %(mm)d   %(mm)d    0
G  %(mm)d  %(mm)d   %(m)d   %(mm)d    0
T  %(mm)d  %(mm)d   %(mm)d   %(m)d    0
N       0       0        0       0    0
"""

def make_matrix(match, mismatch):
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile() as tf:
        tf.write((MATRIX_TEMPLATE % {'mm': mismatch, 'm': match}).encode('ascii'))
        tf.flush()
        return parasail.Matrix(tf.name)

matrix = make_matrix(1, -4)
```

You are completely right, the API doc states: 
"Offset: Identifier of the first update to be returned. Must be greater by one than the highest among the identifiers of previously received updates."
So the offset becomes
get_last_update_id () + 1
Using this method, the message cache becomes unnecessary, as we'll only need to store the id of the last update received. 

My "internal bug" was not actually a bug. The api's 'limit' parameter is set to 100 by default. So should you receive more than 100 messages in less than 24 hours, the last updates won't be added to the getUpdates response. 
There are two ways to solve this problem, either by increasing the 'limit' parameter, or implementing offset in the correct way. I think the last option is most clean, shall I try to fix this tomorrow and submit another pull request?

Messages are processed in a FIFO order from the queue, but due to network latency, Slack response time or message length, concurrent messages can appear in a different order in destination channel than the one you sent them.

I think this behaviour is mostly visible when calling logger multiple times in a row, which should not be a common user case.

If message order is a requirement, you can define `SlackHandler` with a pool size of `1`, so that only one thread will process the queue:

```
slack_handler = SlackHandler('YOUR_WEBHOOK_URL', pool_size=1) 
```

That's... complicated.

So the goal is to have the final URLs in production to contain a hash, so the URL changes if the file changes. Far future cache expirations etc etc.

staticfiles provides this as a feature, via the [post-process step](https://github.com/django/django/blob/38cada7c94f5f73d2d47a0a730ea5d71d266fa2c/django/contrib/staticfiles/storage.py#L208) in some of its backends (including major backends of WhiteNoise). This is called by [collectstatic](https://github.com/django/django/blob/38cada7c94f5f73d2d47a0a730ea5d71d266fa2c/django/contrib/staticfiles/management/commands/collectstatic.py#L126).

Unfortunately, I'm looking at this and I'm not sure how doing this hashing in two commands will mix? Like, will it work ok if it's collecting files in two different commands? Like, I'm looking at projects like StaticfilesPlus to fill in that gap.

@cheves505 have you run into this same error message when you try to `runserver` with a different, unused schema? please check out my issue here if you have: https://github.com/michiya/django-pyodbc-azure/issues/133

Unfortunately django-pyodbc-azure currently doesn't provide support for database schemas as well as the other standard database backends. See the following ticket for more details about database schema support in Django.
https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/6148

As a workaround, you can use a database schema by specifying it as the default schema for your database user. Note that you can't assign `sysadmin` or `db_owner` role to a user that you want to specify its default schema as described in the following page.
https://social.msdn.microsoft.com/Forums/sqlserver/en-US/a1155e64-e53f-4e20-9b1d-4f31e193b14e/default-schema-not-working

T-SQL to create a new user for this would look like the following. Of course you can do this using SQL Server Management Studio.

```
USE MASTER
CREATE LOGIN myuser WITH PASSWORD='password'
GO

USE mydb
GO

CREATE USER myuser FOR LOGIN myuser WITH DEFAULT_SCHEMA = Products
ALTER ROLE db_ddladmin ADD MEMBER myuser

It sounds like you're referring to aiohttp-transmute in particular: that supports more a regex override and similar syntax to transmute's paths:

http://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/web.html#variable-resources

Theoretically transmute's path syntax isn't supposed to mirror any particular framework: it's a coincidence that it matches aiohttp. Of course I understand that doesn't really help you, just giving some background.

For a solution, how about we modify the transmute path syntax to allow arbitrary annotations after colons? basically, anything after the colon is framework-specific metadata. For aiohttp, this would actually make the path syntax identical to normal routes, so

`/test/{param1:regex1}/{param2:regex2}`

would just work.

You could even add a new fence name called `gant` or replace the existing custom fences with one called `mermaid` to handle all mermaid charts: http://facelessuser.github.io/pymdown-extensions/extensions/superfences/#custom-fences.

I don't spend time researching and writing support custom fence examples for people, I leave that up to the user.  The `flow` and `sequence` example I provide by default is there because I use them, and to be an example so others and figure out how to do there own. Hopefully those links can help you get on the correct path.

While I won't code up an entire solution for you beyond what is already available in issue #60, if you do get a little stuck, I can maybe point you in the right direction, but I would expect you to have your current work where I could see it (like hosted on Github).

I use MkDocs, and the default theme for some reason sets the `label` tag to block.  This breaks styling of the default style task lists.  `label` tags *should* be inline, but I can't guarantee this change will be backwards compatible with everyone's current setup.

I will likely put this under a setting toggle that is disabled by default.  This is just to allow a seamless upgrade for people.  For instance, anyone using MkDocs with the "Read the Docs" theme will likely have broken default tasklist style if I don't.

Disallowing arbitrary options is intentional.

However it is fairly easy to add specific behavior. Each option in ``settings.py`` is passed to the compiler:

```python
def __init__(self,
executable=settings.SCSS_EXECUTABLE,
    compass_enabled=settings.SCSS_USE_COMPASS,
    my_new_fancy_option=None):
```
Then just apply this option in ``compile_source`` (and lately also ``compile_file``) by adding it to the ``args`` list which is passed to ``subprocess.Popen``.

I think here are some questions worth considering when designing RDMA-enable MXNet, hopefully getting some insights from MXNet community and byronyi.

1. Shall we build MXNet agnostic to network fabrics (Ethernet, RoCE, Infiniband), or build ones tailored for specific fabrics?
2. Which approach is favored? 1) Add RDMA plugin to ZeroMQ like what @byronyi does to gRPC and TensorFlow; 2) or simply replace ZeroMQ with RoCE message passing semantics.
3. Which parts for MXNet need redesign when porting to RDMA-enable networks? (Personally I think `Van` in pslite is heavily infuenced by ZeroMQ's APIs, and sometimes it looks weird for me.)

@kaxil changes is done

The problem is we have
`airflow_run local` -> `airflow_run raw` -> `task` in terms of process hierarchy.

The local process kicks off the raw process, and then heartbeats periodically, and decides whether or not to kill the task (one example of why it might do so is if the state in the DB has changed)

The raw process checks dependencies, sets the DB state to running, runs the task, then reports success or fail to the DB.

The task is the actual work being done.

The issue is when the local process does the heartbeating, it checks that the state is running, otherwise it kills the task since that means the state was changed externally. However, since the state change happens in a separate thread, we are not sure when it happens. If we assume that the state has already been changed, we will be wrong most of the time. So, the local process keeps a variable called `is_running`, which is set to True iff the heartbeat sees the task in the running state. However, it may never see the task in the running state since it polls the DB (not receives events). 

An example is that a task could be run, and the state could be cleared in the UI before it heartbeats. Now, is_running will never change to True, and the local process will never do the state check. Thus, in this case, the state will be cleared, but the task will continue to run indefinitely.

The other real solution is interprocess communication which is kind of messy, especially when we have different layers due to bash and such. Realistically, the heartbeat process should just be in a multiprocessing thing, but it's a bit hard to refactor now, so if we do decide to go on that route eventually, this is a good first step. This solution of putting the state checks into local is the smallest refactor that doesn't become too large. It's feasible to refactor more of this later, but it is definitely too much for 1 PR, and has questionable value in my opinion.

- We communicate through a TCP socket to get events from an external service through a Go script.

- The events are then wrapped in a celery task before before being queued up, different queue destinations for different types of data. We use RabbitMQ for the broker too. (current being instrumented)
- The task get executed by the work. (Currently being instrumented).

Basically the resource attribution is provided by the defined tags (which are optional) or by the "first part" of the url. I think that this assumption could be too restrictive because a route like: ``/`` or ``/{id}`` MUST have defined a tag to be used and clients could not have permissions to modify specs.

To relax a little this situation I would allow a default tag ``_`` for that cases.
In case the issue is related to the choose of ``_`` (the "default" throwaway python variable) we could think to move it to ``default`` which is the solution adopted by [swagger-ui](http://petstore.swagger.io) (with swagger specs from [http://editor.swagger.io/spec-files/minimal.yaml](http://editor.swagger.io/spec-files/minimal.yaml))

**What we have**

From formlib theres only the ``ISourceQueryView`` interface used:

- Interface https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.formlib/blob/master/src/zope/formlib/interfaces.py#L334
- Usage https://github.com/plone/plone.app.vocabularies/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=ISourceQueryView&type=Code

I seems this is an old definition for what then was expressed by ``zope.browser.interfaces.ITerms`` https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.browser/blob/master/src/zope/browser/interfaces.py#L88

But even this is kind of deprecated, because the "All source implementations will be deprecated" https://github.com/plone/plone.app.vocabularies/blob/ad75d57274ae33ee0d6e8a06e6ce5079428b1ca1/plone/app/vocabularies/configure.zcml#L162

**Proposed Solution:** 
- Really deprecate all source implemenations (let them emit a DeprecationWarning), see http://docs.plone.org/develop/styleguide/deprecation.html
- conditional import from zope.formlib, if not available provide stub Interface
- conditional registration in zcml (only if zope.formlib is available)
- mark code for removal in Plone 6

@sccolbert Sure, but keyword arguments are the same runtime complexity as positional ones.  There might be a slight cost to matching them with keywords during the function call.  If you really care about performance, then the future of fast Python is probably PyPy and we don't know how costly keyword arguments will really be.

The reason cooperative multiple inheritance can't use positional arguments is because you can't be sure which parent you're calling (or if you're even calling one of your direct parents) when you call super, and so you can't know in what order to send your positional arguments.  You have to pass by keyword arguments and let the parents claim the arguments they each want.

@thomasst 
> An idea is to still ensure that the string is valid ISO8601 in its entirety (i.e. you reject strings with an invalid time zone offset), but ignore the timezone information when returning the Python string (or add the offset to the naive datetime so it's always UTC). Would have to check what the actual performance penalty is.

Ah, I see. You are perfectly right. I've profiled it and the performance penalty is almost entirely in the `pytz.FixedOffset` call. So `parse_datetime_unaware` still could validate the entire string, including tz characters for conformance.

> Just to be clear, would parse_datetime still always return a tz-aware datetime (even if there is no TZ information in the datetime string)?

No. `parse_datetime` returns an aware datetime if the input string has tzinfo, and a naive datetime otherwise. **This is not a change from v1.0.7**

I think that clears things up for me. There will be `parse_datetime` and `parse_datetime_as_naive`

|                                    | TZ Aware Input    | TZ Naive Input    |
| ---------------------------------- | ----------------- | ----------------- |
| `parse_datetime()` output          | tz aware datetime | tz naive datetime |
| `parse_datetime_as_naive()` output | tz naive datetime | tz naive datetime |


### Opinion

> Personally, I also prefer to use naive datetimes throughout the code (which are assumed to be in UTC), and most of my use cases actually use parse_datetime_unaware.

That's fine, though I see this as a case where you should be using `parse_datetime`. If somehow a non-UTC datetime were to be introduced into the system, you would notice right away when a comparision between naive and aware datetimes occurred, instead of silently dropping the tzinfo by using `parse_datetime_unaware`. In my mind, `parse_datetime_unaware` should only be used at the "front doors" into your system. By using `parse_datetime` everywhere else, you make sure that there isn't a door you don't know about (and the performance is the same for naive timestamps). Almost all of your uses of cio8601 should be `parse_datetime`, not `parse_datetime_unaware`.

This is why I want to change the name to `parse_datetime_as_naive`. The `as_naive` forces you to acknowledge that this is not just a parser for naive timestamps. If you just needed that, you could simply use `parse_datetime` (with no performance penalty). Instead `parse_datetime_as_naive` is meant for the case where you want and need to strip the tz information for performance reasons. 

Alternative names could be `parse_datetime_ignore_tzinfo` or `parse_datetime_strip_tzinfo`

You would need to duplicate the logic of `request.copy()` to make that work. Just passing in the `environ` may mean that `environ['wsgi.input']` is in the wrong state (i.e. it has been seeked all the way to the end, or it was never made seekable in the first place.) You also want a copy of the environ, because the application may make changes to it that could alter things further down the line, unless you want to explicitly allow that, in which case you need to take extra care with `wsgi.input`.

If it was never made seekable, or is seeked to the end, the second attempt will not have the request body available, so request.POST or accessing request.body might fail or and do the wrong thing.

As long as the factory returns an object that has `__class__` set to itself, `request.copy()` will do the right thing, or you can replicate the behaviour:

```
def tm_tween(request):
    request.make_body_seekable()
    orig_env = request.environ
    factory = registry.queryUtility(IRequestFactory, Request)
    for attempt in attempts:
        # Copy the environment so that we don't allow downstream tweens to affect the original environment
        request = factory(orig_env.copy())
        # Create a copy of the body, so that any modifications to wsgi.input don't affect the next attempt
        request.copy_body()
        return handler(request)
```

@dsanders11 The official support channel is the PayPal Merchant Technical Support website. I believe it is worth opening the issue with Merchant Technical Support because then they can associate the request with specific merchants who want the feature/bug fix. So the problem is not in ```Open Browser ``` or ```Go To``` keywords or at least you do not get a timeout error. Explaining why this works in this way, requires explaining how Selenium2Library and selenium works. The both keywords perform [selenium get](http://selenium-python.readthedocs.org/navigating.html) behind the scenes and the ```selenium get``` listens the ```onload``` event from the browser. That, in theory, should confirm that page is loaded. But in real world, that doesn't never work and all my URL navigation's look like this:
```
| Open Browser | www.url.com |
| Wait Until Page Contains Element | xpath=/something/here | timeout=some number here |
```

Note that this is not a problem in the Selenium2Library or in the selenium implementation, this is the way modern browsers and web applications do work.

As the function is set up now, all you need to do is set a time range and it will pull all the sounding from that day. So for your example, you could write 
````
date_begin = datetime(1997,12,31,0)
date_end = datetime(1997,12,31,23)
station = 'UKM00003026'
df, header = IGRAUpperAir.request_data([date_begin, date_end], station)
````
The resulting dataframe would have all three of those soundings:

````
print(df.date.unique())

['1997-12-31T05:00:00.000000000' '1997-12-31T11:00:00.000000000'
 '1997-12-31T17:00:00.000000000' '1997-12-31T23:00:00.000000000']
````
You could then either use groupby if you have a function you want to apply to each date, or you could just do something like
````
df.loc[df.date.dt.hour == 5,:]
````
if you want to look at an individual sounding.

The length of the dataframe `header` tells you the number of records retrieved, as well as telling you how many levels are reported in each sounding.

````
header.loc[:,['date','number_levels','latitude','longitude']]

````

I played around a bit and this seems to have something to do with how iTerm2 reports Unicode character widths. Setting “Use Unicode Version 9 Widths” in Preferences → Profiles → (The profile you use) → Text fixes it for me.

[iTerm2’s documentation](https://www.iterm2.com/documentation-one-page.html) seems to suggest you should set it unless you have faulty legacy application that malfunctions. New versions of Tmux built with recommended configurations use the new width table.

So to summarise:

* Check the aforementioned checkbox.
* Consider filing an issue to iTerm2 to make this the default.
* If for whatever reason you can’t use the up-to-date width table, set environment variable `PIPENV_HIDE_EMOJIS` to workaround the issue.
Also there is no such package named `docker-compose`, but that is likely not related to the problem.

I'd missed that we weren't already enforcing UTF-8 everywhere - we definitely should be.

As @uranusjr notes, that's straightforward for new files and for ASCII-only files, which means the main cases to handle are going to be existing locale encoded files on \*nix systems and code page encoding files on Windows.

We're helped here by the fact that PyPI itself restricts package names to ASCII-only identifiers, so non-ASCII characters can only appear in comments, and in non-PyPI package names and URLs.

I figure that just fixing the encoding (for files `pipenv` was about to edit anyway) is going to be easier than remembering to preserve the original encoding when writing them back, so I think transparently resolving the problem will be straightforward enough to be worth doing.

(@techalchemy Regarding the UTF-8 mode change in 3.7: that's only the default in the POSIX or C locale, although I think it may be the default on Windows in 3.6+)

@njsmith See the `--pypi-mirror <URL>` sugar suggestion in the last part of my post - if the initial implementation focused solely on that, then the general URL rewriting capability could start out as an internal implementation detail (driven by the fact that "PyPI" has multiple URLs that all ultimately resolve to the same place), and then be considered for exposure as a feature in its own right later on (after it's been confirmed that it's working as desired for the primary `--pypi-mirror` use case).

The intended workflow is that when you first clone a project, you will run `pip install -e .`. You will then edit files, run commands, or otherwise work on the project. After you're done, you will run `pip uninstall .`. Also, running `setup.py` directly will (after a long period of deprecation) be transitioned to implementation-defined behavior. For testing, you will need to run your testing framework in the project directory, with something like `py.test`.

In #789, @ncoghlan said:

> While I agree that situation [where `__init__.py` in the develop package] isn't ideal, I believe it should still work as long as the `__init__.py` does the necessary dance to set its own `__path__` correctly using pkg_resources or pkgutil.

And he's right. The problem is that the presence of the `__init__.py` file in any path representing the namespace causes the PEP 420 find/load behavior to be disabled and since #805, the `-nspkg.pth` behavior is also disabled, so there's nothing left to set up the namespace packages for installed packages.

If I [revert the behavior introduced in #805](/pypa/setuptools/commit/2268fb9887cfea2e28e156bd), the [tests in the develop-nspkg-always branch now pass](https://travis-ci.org/pypa/setuptools/builds/175484039). I think I want to write a test for #805 to demonstrate the use case and expectation.

In the meantime, can @ncoghlan or any one else imagine a way that the `-nspkg.pth` file could be written such that it doesn't interfere with the loading of PEP 420 namespace packages elsewhere in the PYTHONPATH? [This comment](https://github.com/jaraco/rwt/issues/5#issuecomment-238356450) provides some detail on the differences between these packages.

> any updates on when you'll support self-signed certificates?

you can create your own http client based on urllib3, this bug is open for an entirely different reason @leah4kosh 

You are allowed to provide a custom httpClient as shown below following this doc you should be able to use self signed certs with minio-py https://urllib3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/advanced-usage.html#custom-ssl-certificates-and-client-certificates

```py
import urllib3

httpClient = urllib3.PoolManager(
                timeout=urllib3.Timeout.DEFAULT_TIMEOUT,
                        cert_reqs='CERT_REQUIRED',
                        ca_certs='/path/to/my/self-signed/bundle.crt',
                        retries=urllib3.Retry(
                            total=5,
                            backoff_factor=0.2,
                            status_forcelist=[500, 502, 503, 504]
                        )
            )

minioClient = Minio('localhost:9000',
                    access_key='ACCESS_KEY',
                    secret_key='SECRET_KEY',
                    secure=True,
                    http_client=httpClient)
```

Encoding astral characters as 32-bit escape sequences (like `\U0001F600`):
- This method is provided in the YAML spec here: http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#ns-esc-32-bit
- Also, this is the method that PyYAML uses:
  ```py
  >>> import yaml
  >>> print(yaml.dump({'testkey': "🐣 🌼 🌷 🌱 🌳 🍃 🌈"}))
  testkey: "\U0001F423 \U0001F33C \U0001F337 \U0001F331 \U0001F333 \U0001F343 \U0001F308"
  ```
- It is also the method `go-yaml` and the [reference parser](http://ben-kiki.org/ypaste/) uses.
- Here is a reply I received from Leon Timmermans on the YAML core mailing list:
  > Javascript (and a few other languages with UTF-16 implementation details
leaking out) has a tendency to treat such characters as two surrogates
("\uD83D\uDCA9"), instead of as a single character ("\U0001F4A9"). Quite
frankly I think this is unhelpful and wrong, but JSON actually made it a
standard -_-.

I'm in favor of using just `PARLER_LANGUAGES` to determine whether the language code should be rewritten before making the database query. When people want a `fr-ca` -> `fr` -> `en` fallback, it should be explicitly configured in the settings. Otherwise, when only `fr` and `en` are in `PARLER_LANGUAGES`, any variant will be rewritten.

@sunjoomoon @yakky , you can also use `MyModel.objects.translated(name="..", title="..")` which handles the `translations__` prefix on the fly for you.

Note that parler stores the translated fields in a separate table. We don't try to mask that fact as it would increase complexity. Instead, your application will have to deal with this fact when performing queries.

Just throwing this out there:

We can rather simply detect if a given input is one form vs. the other. Instead of a complicated deprecation over years for a bad API decision, we could (I would argue should)

* detect and handle both types of input, emitting a warning for the old type
* detect which type it is, but only handle the new version. Raise an informative error if the old bbox format is used, telling the user how to fix the problem.

Again - I'm usually all for API stability but complicated deprecations guaranteeing multiple code changes are worse than just fixing the problem. In this kind of instance where we can reliably detect which is which, I strongly favor just making the change.

I discussed with @scottpurdy. Here's a summary.

Our code should be organized as:

- Methods that contain almost no logic. They modify the `self`.
- Functions that don't receive a `self`. They receive all of their data explicitly. They can mutate data structures that are explicitly passed in, and/or they can return values.

Following these rules, our code is best as-is. If we chose to make these into non-static methods, we'd need to create new static methods that choose active cells, choose learning segments, etc., so it'd be the same code as today, just more spread around. The long argument lists will still exist, we'll just have more methods.

So I think that's a :-1:.

@breznak Here is a proposed plan for adding additional metrics to swarm over (in this case, speed) and a notion of secondary metrics, in a minimally invasive way:

1. Add an optional `result` (ModelResult) field to MetricsIface.addInstance()
2. Add an optional `duration` field to ModelResult
3. CLAModel.run() sets the `duration` field on ModelResult
4. MetricsManager.update(result) passes the ModelResult in when calling Metric.addInstance()
5. SpeedMetric.addInstance() can use the `duration` field in the ModelResult instance to compute average duration as its metric value
6. Modify ModelRunner to accept in __init__ a list of metric key patterns (instead of a single `_optimizeKeyPattern`), and store these as a list of metrics to optimize for
7. Modify ModelRunner.__checkIfBestCompletedModel() to use the list of metrics to optimize for, and check them in order, using later metrics only when earlier metrics result in a tie
8. Repeat step 2 and step 5 when adding other types of data for metrics to use

@kaplun: for configurability, I agree that there's a defined set. Probably, it needs elaboration, but this might depend on the model further down the road. One can treat permanent loans like a collection of a library,  maybe this is a better approach. 

For the defined shelf: My musing was in the direction of item = one bibliographic item (=record), which holds several of your items. I think now it's clear to me what your item is. Still more status info (thinking in `876 $j` in Marc) is locked even to your items:  you're right , that "on shelf" is the status, however, Marc `876` always goes with `852` defining which shelf.

@tiborsimko: for individual articles in a proceedings volume, if you want to make them available for lending, you'd have individual bibliographic records and add holdings to _them_, not the parent. You'd need this anyway for retrieval on the users side. I agree that I'd also have a parent record as a common bracket around them (requires record linking), but the holdings, status and such would live on the articles, not on the parent entity. Very similar are multi volume works which are _very_ common. E.g. you'd catalogue every volume of the _Britannica Biographical encyclopedia of artists_ and not just _Encyclopedia Britannica, 4 Volumes A-Z_ (E.g. http://gso.gbv.de/DB=2.1/PPNSET?PPN=523994168 for the parent, http://gso.gbv.de/DB=2.1/SET=7/TTL=1/FAM?PPN=523994168 for the children). Another very similar thing where it is quite obvious, would be a book series. Surely, you'd catalogue every book and the series would only be used as the bracket.

* Besides the reference works @tiborsimko mentioned, borrowing times would better not live on the item, but on the borrower(s' role) as also commented by you below. E.g. if you have a lot of students at your lab it might well be that you give them a lending period of only a week (probably they are only there for the 4 weeks of your regular lending period...) while a regular scientist of the lab gets 4 weeks. Or in a university setting: students get 4 weeks while university staff gets "unlimited till claimed by someone."

* For the `LIBRARIES` class there is definitely some sub location (named _collection_ in current `bibcirculation`, if I understood it correctly). This could handle the above mentioned reference works: if in sub location "reference" it follows that nobody can borrow it,  unless role allows to (probably staff can get it anyway as they are assumed "on campus and available all the time").

* For additional inspiration I think Koha is probably indeed a pretty good starting point as AFAIK it models most use cases.

Still, even if you might not love it (you don't have to), I'd check into the Marc Holdings (852)[http://www.loc.gov/marc/holdings/hd852.html] and (876/878)[http://www.loc.gov/marc/holdings/hd876878.html]. I discussed the issue of cataloging with @katringrosse, @CLSi and also our local man at the front @mahncm.  It crystallized that the current procedure of doing `bibedit` the click through the holdings stuff is not very convenient. For our case I thus implemented a simple one way sync from Marc `852`/`876` to the internal tables as this is a much more streamlined work flow for the cataloguers. I added some `field-templates` to ease up things (here it would be nice if a field template could add two fields at once) and currently try to integrate a few simple `kb`s to keep typing to a minimum. The general comment was: "I'm in `bibedit` anyway for cataloguing, no need to leave it. Make addition of holdings to happen there and with just adding fields." My current implementation uses (indicator 1 == `7` signifies: source in `$2` as usual) uses:

* 8527_

subfield | value          | example
---------|----------------|--------
2        | Source         | DESY
a        | Library        | DESY Hamburg Library
b        | collection     | Permanent Loan
c        | shelfmark      | C Loe
h        | classification | C
p        | barcode        | 082932
t        | item number    | 1
z        | public note    | <description>

* 8767_

At DESY NSK is a number similar to an accession number. But the real accession number is the the barcode (in general accession number and barcode differ). I don't like the `$h` yet as it should actually live on the users role but I fear I can't do that in invenio 1.x:

subfield | value          | example
---------|----------------|--------
2        | Source         | DESY
a        | NSK            | 47538
d        | Accession date | 20141120000000.0
p        | barcode        | 082932
h        | loan period    | 4 weeks / 1 week / reference
j        | availability   | available/missing
t        | item number    | 1

I had a similar issue. I think there's two ways to solve it:

1) In memory db
```python
app = Flask('testapp')
app.config.update(SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI='sqlite://', TESTING=True,)
```

2) Set instance path to temporary directory
```python
@pytest.fixture()
def script_info(request):
    instance_path = tempfile.mkdtemp()
    app = Flask(__name__, instance_path=instance_path)

    def teardown():
        shutil.rmtree(instance_path)

    request.addfinalizer(teardown)
    return app
```

Probably (2) is the best, since for Travis we might test both SQLite, Postgres and MySQL so (1) wouldn't work in that case.

We think the right way to fix this is to add an additional configuration option to the `show_grid` function, similar to the existing `remote_js=False` option, that would allow the user to specify the base_url for the server.

Since `base_url` is a config option that you'd probably just want to set once rather than on every call to `show_grid`, you could do something like add a configure function that a user could call once with options like this: `qgrid.configure(remote_js=False, base_url='my_base_url')`  The function could use a dictionary internally to keep track of the configured options, and use them as the default options for subsequent calls to `show_grid`.
