Ditching unicode inheritance from Namespace seems tempting. I am less convinced about ditching it from everything. More because it will be a lot of work and will introduce a lot of bugs that we don't really have the developer resources to fix. Maybe when @joernhees has finished his PhD? :) 

I wonder what we gain by having things inherit from unicode code. Off the top of my head I cannot think of that many things this enables that I actively use? 

Pros:
* it is neat :) 
* You can compose URIRefs easily (but a `Namespace` is safer)
* You can slice URIRefs (but we could implement slicing anyway?)

Cons: 
 * Python is pretty good at transparently converting to str/unicode with `__str__`, `__unicode__` anyway. 
 * No concensus on what operators on objects should return. (For literals there is value/lexical space: https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/pull/451) (For URIRefs we did this: https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/pull/258, perhaps hastily - I was bitten by composing a string for logging with `+` ( yes, yes, I know ... ) and URIRef tried to validate it )

Thanks for the comments Mike.  I think I've at least briefly responded to all of them now.

I played around a lot the weekend-before-last trying to speed things up, and made some progress.  I think the biggest improvement was solving for an actual maxk instead of just asserting the conservative one derived from Airy.  A lot of the ideas I tried to gain speed led to unit test failures.  I seemed to run in to `check_basic_k` failures a lot, and also failures to find a radius that satisfied `folding_threshold`.  

I left some added infrastructure in place for now that I was using to check up on various approximations as I optimized.  I'm assuming you'll want to try your hand at this too now; please feel free excise any of the additional cruft as you see fit.  I'm not attached to it, but didn't see any reason to delete it before you had a chance to look.

Well, that constant term doesn't make a whole lot of sense.  Maybe the math works out that way, but that means there is a delta function sitting in the center of the PSF.  We could just define a modified von Karman that is a real von Karman minus the delta function.  So D(r) would just have the second term in the brackets without the Gamma(5/6)/2^1/6 term.

Is there any reason we expect the real atmosphere to actually produce a nice Kolmogorov-y thing plus a faint delta function at the center?

I think I got this working now, and it did turn out to be most transparent.  I actually decided to go with photons/nm/cm^2/s instead of photons/nm/m^2/s since this is more cgs-consistent, and share/vega.txt is already in cgs units, for example.

I did run into a bit of trouble with one of the wfirst unit tests comparing the expected WFIRST photon flux given an SDSS-normalized SED.  I had to degrade the tolerance of the test from 10% to 15%.  I think this may have been a case where the previous code was actually not doing the right thing though, and may have even passed by virtue that WFIRST and SDSS have similar collecting areas.  I like the current test better.

Finally, this change has made me aware of some other dimensionally inconsistent things.  For example, since I'm now hiding the collecting area and exposure time in the `Bandpass` (btw, I'm now hiding the collecting area and exposure time in a `Bandpass` object), multiplying a `Bandpass` by an `SED` no longer yields an `SED`  (photons/nm/cm^2/s  != photons/nm).  Similarly, I think some of the logic in the Chromatic deconvolution/autoconvolution/etc doesn't make sense.  For example, ChromaticDeconvolution has a line: `self.SED = lambda w: 1./obj.SED(w)`, which is dimensional nonsense.

I think the issue here is that we effectively have two types of `ChromaticObject`s that aren't currently being distinguished.  Some chromatic objects, such as `galsim.Gaussian() * galsim.SED()`, effectively represent a profile with units of [photons / nm / cm^2 / s / arcsec^2], while others, such as `galsim.OpticalPSF` only represent a profile with units [1/arcsec^2].  To make an image, we generally convolve one of the former types (SED'd ChromaticObjects) with some number of the latter types (non SED'd ChromaticObjects).  I don't think it ever makes sense to convolve two or more SED'd ChromaticObjects, or add together an SED'd and non-SED'd ChromaticObject.  Similarly, ChromaticDeconvolution/AutoConvolution/etc only make sense for the non-SED'd ChromaticObjects.

Probably we can keep distinguish the two types above and make things consistent just by using a default `ChromaticObject.SED = None` instead of `SED = galsim.SED('1')`  and appropriate introspection for when `self.SED is None`.  I think I'll try this out today, but I'm curious, @rmandelb, if you have thoughts about this since I think we've (politely) collided on related issues in the past. :).

Bonjour Hakim,

Sur mes-aides, pour ne pas se placer en porte à faux vis à vis des administrations, nous avons choisi de ne jamais recalculer le montant des prestations que l'utilisateur déclare. Si quelqu'un déclare toucher 500€ de RSA au mois de février, nous considérerons dans nos calculs qu'il touchera aussi 500€ au mois de mars, et n'afficherons pas le RSA dans la page de résultats.

L'implémentation de ce choix est faite côté mes-aides, et ne devrait pas avoir d'impact côté OpenFisca. 

Côté OpenFisca, si par exemple vous fournissez la valeur du RSA pour février, et que vous demandez la valeur du RSA pour mars, a priori il devrait refaire le calcul et ne pas vous renvoyer ce que vous avez donné en entrée. Si vous constatez un comportement anormal, merci de nous indiquer les `openfisca-france` et de `openfisca-web-api` que vous utilisez, ainsi qu'un exemple de requête JSON permettant de mettre en évidence le problème.

Par contre, si vous fournissez la valeur du RSA pour février, et que vous demandez la valeur du RSA pour février, oui, c'est bien la même valeur qui vous sera retourné.

I misspoke above when I said TimeGates return a Memento-Datetime header (if it were present a client couldn't tell an archived 302 from a live 302); as you mentioned they (optionally but typically) provide the Memento-Datetime *value* in the Link header.  The exception as you note is the 200-style CN (pattern 2.2), but that's a different animal altogether (the most typical problem is that the browser won't update its URI with the value in Content-Location).  Pattern 2.2 is defined and possible, but 200-style CN has pretty much fallen out of favor.  

I think I understand what you're requesting, but (as you suggest above) perhaps instead of modifying MementoClient(), it would be better to have separate function(s) that disaggregates the steps.  We kind of have something in that direction with "check_native_timegate=False", which short-circuits the HEAD on URI-R.  Given that you can have X redirects before you finally hit a (real) TimeGate, and Y redirects between a TimeGate and a memento, perhaps the best thing is to have a function that takes as an input the previous HTTP response and then does the next (and only the next) step while returning the resulting HTTP response (so it can be used as an input to the next call).  

Essentially, MementoClient() implements "curl -L" and the proposed function simply removes the "-L" flag and requires an explicit call to take the next step.  This would allow an application (with all appropriate caveats) to be able to bail out of the Memento chain at any point without committing to chasing it down to some terminal response code (and maybe with some return values indicating its best guess as to where it is in the chain, e.g. "past a TimeGate but haven't hit a memento yet").  Or maybe it could allow for resuming a stalled / checkpointed Memento request chain without having to start from the beginning.

Although it was my understanding that the purpose of this lib was to hide all the Memento chain business in a full up client.  Perhaps it should be a separate library, but it's also nice to have something that does the right thing with Accept-Datetime and all the resulting headers.

There are a few issues here:

* you should rather not allow Django to serve your `favicon.ico`
* you should make your 404.html as simple as possible so you don't get further errors when for example the database dies
* `django-cookie-law` should rather raise a warning than an exception when request context is not found in the response.

The V4 signature issue can be solved by setting the following in your ~/.aws/config file,

```
[default]
        region = eu-central-1
        s3 = 
          use_accelerate_endpoint = true
          signature_version = s3v4
```

The will force the signature to V4. Other options can be found [here](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/topic/s3-config.html)

No. This is totally different. HTTPIE gets a timeout even when specifying a bigger timeout period. I am getting a timeout even though my resulting Lambda function does complete the process successfully.

However, I believe I know the reason.

It turns out that with Chalice you can set the timeout for the Lambda function (up to 300 seconds) and in HTTPIE you can specify its timeout. However, API Gateway has a hardcoded timeout of 30 seconds, so a timeout longer than 30 seconds in Chalice has no real effecting API Gw. The Lambda function will continue to work (up to the timeout set in Chalice <= 300), but API Gateway will return a timeout at 30 seconds.

Unless the API Gateway timeout can be set via a parameter (like I said, it seems to be hardcoded today), working with Chalice calls longer than 30 seconds will always timeout. It kind of defeats the purpose of being able to set a longer timeout in Chalice.

> We will all need to follow the routing as it exists now on gcloud-node:

It sounds to me like there might be a way to structure things so that we don't need to have a special path structure, and can all share one big JSON document. Not saying we *must* but... it's something I don't want to write off yet.

> - /service -> Constructor and methods for base class (e.g. Storage)
> - /service/sub-class -> Constructor and methods for (e.g. Storage/File)
> - /service/sub-class?method=methodName -> Single method on sub-class (e.g. Storage/File#getFiles)

This will probably work for Ruby, but Python doesn't create a `Storage` class, it creates a `storage.client.Client` class. So for us the hierarchy is really more like....:

- /service -> Python module (ie `gcloud.storage` which lines up with the service)
- /service/concept -> Python class inside the module (ie, `gcloud.storage.bucket.Bucket`)
- /service/concept?method=methodName -> single method on the Python class

> We can definitely filter and combine our Dox JSON into one file of the essentials. And maybe key it by the same router hierarchy?

I'm a big fan of that. For us, we'll need everything each step of the way because `gcloud.storage`, `gcloud.storage.bucket`, and `gcloud.storage.bucket.Bucket` are all things that can hold descriptions, examples, and methods.

```javascript
{
  "gcloud": { /* Python module (ie, gcloud, gcloud.credentials, gcloud.storage, etc) */
    "description": "...",
    "type": "module",
    "example": "...",
    "methods": { /* Module-level methods (ie, gcloud.credentials.get_credentials)*/ }
  },
  "gcloud.storage": { /* Python module */
    "description": "...",
    "type": "module",
    "example": "...",
    "methods": {}
  },
  "gcloud.storage.bucket": { /* Python module */
    "description": "...",
    "type": "module",
    "example": "...",
    "methods": {}
  },
  "gcloud.storage.storage.bucket.Bucket": { /* Python class */
    "description": "...",
    "type": "class",
    "example": "...",
    "methods": {}
  }
}
```

Thoughts?

I don't think so.  The code (linked above) is obviously there and working... but it's currently part of `mystic`.  It probably should be moved to `multiprocess` or `pathos.multiprocessing`.

`pathos.pools.ProcessPool` and `pathos.multiprocessing.ProcessingPool` are the same... (the latter is just the deprecated API).

If you think it's a memory issue, it might be worthwhile to try `pathos.pools._ProcessPool`.
The difference there is that the `ProcessPool` isn't terminated after a `close`, which *can* lead to memory leaks when there's too much reuse.  The `_ProcessPool` has an exact match to `multiprocessing.Pool`, so there's a `maxtasksperchild` setting and the like.

我觉得如果支持ss中继还是不错的， 我这边有个需求就是中间有个自己的代理提供ss来进行流量过滤， 不能用单纯的tcp转发流量，有这个几个考虑:
1.   客户端比较傻，不能进行高级的流量区分， 比如我希望拦截http流量（不信任ss供应商, 怕流量抓包)。
2.  中间代理不能走socks5代理 或者 http代理（https需要搞证书，比较麻烦，跑个sserver容易多了), 因为比如在一些情况下（比如共用wifi），我希望到中间代理的流量也加密， 然后在中间代理那边经过规则判断之后是走代理还是直接连接目标服务器。

好像主实现都不支持中继， 所以我打算用tsocks包一层，｛｝保住的部分在中间服务器上
`dst_sserver <-> ｛ sslocal <-> filter <-> tsocks <-> sserver｝ <-> sslocal(mobile device) `
Vultr+ss+浙江移动宽带同今天下午出现异常，VPS可ping通可SSH登陆，但ss 客户端一直提示timed out，服务端日志无连接记录。

我原以为ss-local和ss-server之间只有一个连接，最近研究了源码之后才发现，socks5客户端每发起一个连接，ss-local就向ss-server发起一个连接，然后发送数据。它们是一一对应的。发送和接收都是由这个连接负责。这样的好处是每个socks5连接都有一个对应的 socket，它们各自负责收发各自的数据收发，互不打扰。这样的话 ss-local 就不需要考虑收到的数据是那个socks5的。

但是这样一来就会出现 @wobushizhanghua 所说的 **TCP 干扰和TCP握手时间** 的问题，同时也在无形之中增加了系统的开销。那要怎么解决这个问题呢 ？连接复用当然是个好办法，但是如果不更改shadowsocks的协议的话似乎很难实现。发送的话没有问题，但是ss-local在接收到ss-server的数据之后不知道这个数据是的socks5客户端的哪个连接发起的，也就无法转发给socks5客户端了。

我的想法是 在初始化的时候让 ss-local 和 ss-server 建立一个连接，ss-local 收到socks5客户端的数据之后，在shadowsocks协议的基础上把请求的来源ip和端口加到要发送的数据中，然后加密、发送给ss-server。ss-server接收到数据之后对数据进行解密，从中获取到请求的来源ip和端口（这个来源ip和端口是socks5客户端的），然后用来源ip和端口作为参数实例化一个类，接着建立一个新的连接访问目标服务器。之后的数据收发跟现有的流程一样。ss-server在收到目标服务器返回的数据后，用类似的方法把之前的来源ip和端口添加到要发送给ss-local的数据中。ss-local收到数据之后再次从中获取到之前的来源ip和端口，然后根据这个ip和端口把数据发送给socks5客户端。至此通信完毕！之后的socks5连接都共用一个ss-local→ss-server 连接。

这个方案需要更改tcprelay.py 和 udprelay.py 文件，把来源ip和端口添加到shadowsocks协议中，并且需要更改 tcprelay.py 和 udprelay.py 的逻辑（主要是修改ss-local相关的逻辑），相当于是大改了。如果更改之后真的效果显著的话，那可以考虑尝试更改一下。

不知 @wobushizhanghua 和各位高手还有没有什么更好的方案？

## [Lightsocks](https://github.com/gwuhaolin/lightsocks)没有被识别出来
由于 Lightsocks 服务端默认监听在 7448 端口，导致该端口已经被封，而不是 Lightsocks 已经被 GWF 特征分析出来了。这说明我们当前在讨论的内容正在被监视着。
**你只需要换一个非 7448 的端口就能正常使用，亲测！**
或者，你升级下 Lightsocks 版本即可，我已经将 Lightsocks 服务端的默认监听端口7448改成随机生成。


## Lightsocks 原理
Lightsocks 的原理是通过一个 256 大小的 byte 数组在 byte 层面对数据流进行混淆，混淆的方法非常简单高效，而不是通过加密的方式去混淆。

## 申明
在此申明：Lightsocks  只是一个网络混淆代理工具，和翻墙没有任何关系，纯粹出于技术学习和探索。Lightsocks 只是我学习 Golang 的一个练手项目，开放出来是为了和大家探讨和交流技术。

I just pushed up the current iteration of Pants consuming the native code toolchain I'm developing so that we can discuss real code. It has no tests now, which is going to change very soon. I think the ExecutionEnvironmentMixin is a step in the right direction before we  can get into composable v2 Snapshots, etc. I think separating the individual tools in the toolchain (gcc, clang, binutils) from their usage with something like the contextmanager here is the easiest way to avoid having to put on band-aids after the fact. I left a couple longish comments intentionally, those will absolutely be removed when this is merged.

Also, as I build out testing for this toolchain now it might end up being much easier to have (on Linux) gcc, clang, and ld all be installed into a single subdirectory of `~/.cache/pants`, and merging the `bin/`, `include/`, `lib/` etc of each. Not only does this avoid having to specify e.g. include and lib directories when we invoke the compiler, it would also then be much easier to try out compiling locally in a literal chroot -- I'm not sure that's a goal for any of us but it would definitely make it easier to avoid environment pollution spilling incompatible versions of glibc into your binaries, for example.

@fhieber 
Regarding closing the summary writer gracefully. Here is the analysis of several situations.
1. If you flush every time after calling `add_xxx`, there is almost no risk of data loss. However, this is not the recommended way of using the logger since `flush` would actually block the main thread till writing to the file finishes, while `add_xxx` only puts the item into the queue for logging, which is much faster than directly writing to files, and a separate thread would do the real logging work.
2. If some hard crash happens in the middle of training job without throwing any exceptions, for example, a segmentation fault caused by C++ code (probably caused by improper implementation), items in the queue for logging will be lost.
3. If some crash happens and raises an exception in Python, you would either need to wrap your code in the scope of `with SummaryWriter` or use a `try...except: sw.close()` block to prevent data loss. In the former case, you don't need to call `sw.close()` explicitly as the `with` statement would do that for you.

I was looking into why multiprocessing requires fork & exec and not just fork.
I was also looking at what happens with gunicorn only forking.

By adding log statements to `prometheus_client.core` I noticed the below (which may be unrelated but it is suspicious to me).
Logging code added to `prometheus_client.core`:
```python
import logging
log = logging.getLogger('test')
def _MultiProcessValue(__pid=os.getpid()):
    ...
    log.error("~~~" "PID: %s", pid)

    class _MmapedValue(object):
        ...
        def __init__(...):
            ...
            self._lock = Lock()
            log.error("~~~" "PId2: %s => %s", pid, os.getpid())
        ...
```

In order to configure gunicorn to clean up live gauges, the gunicorn configuration module imports `prometheus_client.multiprocess`.
This in turn imports `prometheus_client.core` which runs `_ValueClass = _MultiProcessValue()` here https://github.com/prometheus/client_python/blob/ce7f2978499dbd24e1028ef8966e50f374f51f5a/prometheus_client/core.py

At this stage, `os.getpid()` is executed in the (gunicorn) master process.
When the process forks, children inherit the pre-initialised `prometheus_client.core` module with the master pid and never update to use their own pid.

By removing the import in the config module (I removed cleanup for testing, a better way is needed) we can see that `prometheus_client.core` is now left uninitialised in the master process and each worker initialises it with a different pid instead of all using the master pid.

Is it possible to re-work `prometheus_client.multiprocess` to avoid the top-level import of `prometheus_client.core`?
I know that nested imports are bad, maybe move the `MultiProcessCollector` to a separate file that can be skipped while configuring the master process.
Could we at least change 'The official Python 2 and 3 client for Prometheus.' to 'The official Python 2 and 3 client for use with Prometheus.' ? I think it would make a big difference.

This does set me thinking though. In the XML Semantics section, we need to discover and describe a few things:

* What, if any, is the allowable "nesting" behavior of bookmarks? In particular, can a bookmark appear entirely within another bookmark? Can bookmarked areas overlap or must they appear in strictly nested order?

* What happens if a `bookmarkEnd` element appears before its matching `bookmarkStart` element in a document? Is this allowable (like the user selected a block of text from bottom to top) or does it perhaps trigger a repair error (or just get ignored and perhaps confuse a developer).

  I don't think we'll have to do anything special to avoid problems with this because ending a bookmark requires a open bookmark as a parameter, which by definition means the start precedes the end.

* What happens if two `bookmarkEnd` elements appear with the same `id`? (I.e. the bookmark is closed "twice").

I'm thinking the `Document.end_bookmark()` method needs to check for the "already closed" condition and raise an exception in that case.

```python
def end_bookmark(self, bookmark):
    """Close *bookmark* at current end of document."""
    if bookmark.is_closed:
        raise ValueError('cannot end closed bookmark')
    self._element.add_bookmarkEnd(bookmark.id)
```

Okay, regarding the parentage aspect, I'm thinking the key parent elements to start with are `w:body` and `w:p`. Maybe the table ones come in later, I'll have to noodle that a bit more, but table cells contain paragraphs, so you'd already be able to put one inside a cell, and I can't think of any super-common uses for a bookmarked cell, row, or table at the moment.

One possibility is to implement the bookmark functionality as a mixin, so it can be added to multiple "locations" without duplicating the code. That would also provide a uniform interface, regardless of the parent object. Maybe we call that `BookmarkParentMixin` or maybe something a little better. That approach would also make it a lot easier to add different "parents" later on.

Thanks @Tores76. This is an interesting and useful finding. Your hypothesis makes sense to me; Word has access to the header whenever it has the document and I suppose it's reasonable they would place those ids into the same scope of uniqueness.

We don't have programmatic access to the header XML document(s) yet, so we wouldn't be able to search there yet. The artificial increase in ID for new ones we add makes sense to me though. I'll bet that's a pretty robust approach that would work in all cases we're likely to encounter.

I'll leave this open and put it on the short list for things we can fix next time we're in there.

I am just wondering about adding `six` though. I agree that our own `compat` file is getting a bit busy, and there are various kludges in the code to deal with compatibility (e.g. metaclasses), so now would be a good time to add an external library to handle that for us.

In my view, however, [python-future](http://python-future.org) might be a neater solution. It is also widely-used, but targets only Python2.6 and greater (which is what we need) so it avoids cruft dealing with earlier python versions.  The best part, for me, is that it enables you to write clean and normal python3 code, which will then just work on earlier versions.  In other words, you can just import a module as usual, without having to remember to import from `six.moves`. It also includes more back ported functionality, so we can use more modern constructs without worrying so much.  Have a look at http://python-future.org/faq.html#what-is-the-relationship-between-future-and-six

What do you think?

Finally something that points to the problem!

The environment regarding virtualenvs is actually correctly set. The problem is this:

On a normal environment with a virtualenv activated:

```
which python
/home/user/.virtualenvs/venv/bin/python
```

But with execSync:


```
which python
/usr/bin/python
```

So actually this works:

```
  execSync("kolibri manage webpack_json --outputfile " + webpack_json_tempfile, {env: process.env});
```

And this breaks unless `kolibri/dist` is populated:

```
execSync("python -m kolibri manage webpack_json --outputfile " + webpack_json_tempfile, {env: process.env});
```

This is one of the [non-standard layouts](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1dpKGD_nMzYmqa82EmLdLGMZTIodJ6MJFLS63ijSr7UM/edit#) so there's no one correct answer. As I mentioned in that doc, I think it's best to define these layouts in terms of [proportional grids](https://purecss.io/grids/) and our [standard breakpoints](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/develop/kolibri/core/assets/src/mixins/responsive-window.js#L26). Note in that file that [Material design gives guidance](https://material.io/guidelines/layout/responsive-ui.html#responsive-ui-grid) on details like what column granularity to use at different breakpoint sizes, and what gutter widths to use.

Basically, blocks are specified as taking up some integer multiple of a column width. Within a block, text might wrap as necessary.

Just for sake of argument, I would expect a responsive layout spec to look like something this:

-----

## > 600 px

8 col granularity:

```
|       |       |       |       |       |       |       |       |
```

layout:

```
| title                         |       | lang  | search        |
```

widths:

* title - 4 x 1/8; spacer - 1 x 1/8; lang - 1 x 1/8; search - 2 x 1/8


## 480 - 600 px

4 col granularity:

```
|               |               |               |               |
```

layout:

```
| title                                                         |
| lang          | search                                        |
```

widths:

* title - 4 x 1/4
* lang - 1 x 1/4; search - 3 x 1/4

## < 480 px

4 col granularity:

```
|               |               |               |               |
```

layout:

```
| title                                                         |
| lang                                          |               |
| search                                                        |
```

widths:

* title - 4 x 1/4
* lang - 3 x 1/4;  spacer - 1 x 1/4
* search - 4 x 1/4

@graingert:

> You don't need to be up to date with the spec, or have documentation for semver 1.0.0. You just need to be ready to bump the version 2.0.0 when you add a breaking change.

The problem is defining what a breaking change is when you're in a language like Python where *everything* is public. Pretty sure our options are essentially:

* Underscore prefix everything in one fell swoop and hope people believe anything prefixed isn't a stable API. (Which, to be fair, probably mostly suffices!) As an aside, doing much of this is a large part of what caused breakage in the last release.

* Increment major version number quickly by underscore prefixing things when they're next changed. (And likely this will happen even if the above is done because some will get missed initially despite best intentions.)

* Document everything that is intended to be a stable API (and this doesn't have to be perfect documentation; at some level even a simple list of "these APIs are stable" would suffice here). 

As for the naming of the package, I'd argue the "5" is misleading as well, but renaming the package almost certainly causes more pain than it's worth, and there's already a `html` package on PyPI.

@joernhees:

The problems with setuptools are its own messes; see [this](https://gsnedders.github.io/python-marker-test/results.html) for a summary of the different behaviours of different versions of setuptools on different Python implementations… Notably, none of `platform_python_implementation`, `platform.python_implementation`, or `python_implementation` work across all commonly used setuptools versions on all Python implementations. We now have a really, really, really hideous monkey-patch for setuptools in our `setup.py` from https://github.com/html5lib/html5lib-python/pull/285 to make it work everywhere.

See the `output = "&" + "".join(charStack)` on line 173, and line 216–223. Because none of the characters on the stack are characters that will change the tokeniser state, we directly append them instead of unconsuming them and reconsuming them, which is black-box equivalent. The validator.nu parser (and as a result the programmatic conversion of that which Gecko uses) doesn't have *any* unconsume and implements the whole tokeniser as a DFA.

I don’t have definitive answers, but I did some more investigation and made some progress that I wanted to document here:

I have noticed a couple of things - 
1. This happens more when the number of assets for a course is very large.
2. This happens more when I am on a high bandwidth connection which means HTTP requests are happening faster.

One smoking gun is a lot of messages in the log which say: `Resetting dropped connection: www.coursera.org`. This indicates that connections that are (maybe) being kept alive by the urllib3 connection pool are being closed by Coursera, and are being re-established by urllib3. This is an indication of some sort of rate-limiting/abuse prevention logic. 

I decided to add a delay in the `get_page` method in network.py by doing:

```
sleep(0.2)
```

I tried values from 0.05 (50ms)  to 0.2(200ms), and 0.2 worked best and caused the least problems.

Another option that I have not tried is that we could consider disabling keepalive in `get_session` method in coursera_dl.py by doing:

```
session.keep_alive = false
```

Maybe a combination of adding a delay (maybe specified by a CLI flag) and disabling keep-alive might help solve this problem.

@meejah Very interesting!

Here is what comes to my mind to look deeper:

* breakdown CPU into user/system (http://netdata.firehol.org/ - https://github.com/firehol/netdata/wiki/Installation)
* measure syscall rate (http://linux.die.net/man/1/strace or systemtap)
* measure interrupt rate (is that loopback?)
* what's the JITted code doing? (vmprof, Linux perf)

To establish a lower bound rgd CPU load, we could measure this: have a plain Twisted TCP server that sends out 1 octet pseudo-pings to the 20k connected clients.

My expectation: it'll be mostly system CPU and roughly scale with effective syscall rate

syscall rate is the ultimate barrier with networking code when using regular Posix socket API. There is no way of getting beyond that barrier (as of now, as far as I know) without switching to a radical different design: kernel-bypass networking.

The reason is the inherit limitation of the Posix socket API design: you can't write (different) bytes to different sockets in one syscall.

I emailed Bryan McCann, and he gave me some info about the hyperparameters he used. I added them to the training config, and the model seems to work swimmingly now.

It achieves a best validation accuracy of `52.86`, which is quite close to his published result in the CoVe paper (`53.5`) and a decent improvement over the baseline in the ELMo paper (`51.4`).

Evaluating the best validation checkpoint on the test set yields an accuracy of `53.21`, which seems fairly competitive to me. For reference, these are the test set scores and comparisons in the CoVe paper:

![screen shot 2018-03-27 at 11 59 05 pm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7272031/38013697-e919c02e-321a-11e8-8171-22129220a8f5.png)

Some caveats:
- This model doesn't have batchnorm. I'm happy to add batchnorm, but not sure what the best way to do so is, API-wise.
- The above scores are on a single run. SST is pretty small, so it'd be nice to repeat the experiment several times and use that to get a figure --- however, I need the GPUs for something else now :)

Hi James, thanks for the detail response. One question:
[{'c': {'d': 'AAAA'}}]   --> search('c.d') -> None
Searching for "c.d" should be "AAAA" right? why is it None should that not be 'AAAA'? looks like search "a.*" returns an array.

Is there a search expression, which can give "AAAA" here?
For example:>>> c = {'a': {'b': {'c': {'d': 'AAAA'}}, 'b2': {'c': {'d': 'DDDD'}}}}
>>> jmespath.search ("a.b.c.d", c )
'AAAA'
>>> jmespath.search ("a.b2.c.d", c )
'DDDD'
>>> jmespath.search ("a.*.c", c )
[{'d': 'AAAA'}, {'d': 'DDDD'}]
>>>
For the variable c, is there any JSON Path search which gives me ["AAAA", "DDDD"] back? Like search expression "a.*.c.d"
 Regards, Majeed Arni

      From: James Saryerwinnie <notifications@github.com>
 To: jmespath/jmespath.py <jmespath.py@noreply.github.com> 
Cc: MajeedArni <marni@sbcglobal.net>; Author <author@noreply.github.com>
 Sent: Sunday, May 14, 2017 8:50 PM
 Subject: Re: [jmespath/jmespath.py] Wildcard issue (#131)
   
Hi, anything that's after the * is part of the projection that's created from *. Conceptually, it's parsed as a.*.(c) and a.*.(c.d). So breaking that down, we have:>>> jmespath.search("a", {"a": { "b": { "c": { "d": "AAAA"}}}})
{'b': {'c': {'d': 'AAAA'}}}
>>> jmespath.search("a.*", {"a": { "b": { "c": { "d": "AAAA"}}}})
[{'c': {'d': 'AAAA'}}]
So for a.*.c, it's evaluating the expression c against each element in the list of [{'c': {'d': 'AAAA'}}]:[{'c': {'d': 'AAAA'}}]   --> search('c') -> {'d': 'AAAA'}

# So the final result is a list of each search result:

[{'d': 'AAAA'}]
That means that a.*.c.d would execute as this:[{'c': {'d': 'AAAA'}}]   --> search('c.d') -> None

# And in a projection, a `None` value is excluded from the resulting list,
# so the final result would be an empty list:
[]
More info about that here: http://jmespath.org/specification.html#wildcard-expressionsLet me know if any of that isn't clear or you have any more questions.—

Hi, the arguments are backwards :).  It's modeled after the `re.search` argument order which is `search(pattern, data)`.  Also, the `data` is expected to be the parsed JSON and not a string.  So in our example:

```
import json
import jmespath
a = json.loads("""
{"eventVersion": "1.05", "eventID": "123", "eventTime": "", "requestParameters": {"ipPermissions": {"items": [{"prefixListIds": {}, "fromPort": 22, "ipRanges": null, "groups": null, "toPort": 22, "ipProtocol": "tcp", "ipv6Ranges": {"items": [{"cidrIpv6": "::/0"}]}}]}, "groupId": "sg-xxxx"}, "eventType": "AwsApiCall", "responseElements": {"_return": true}, "awsRegion": "", "eventName": "", "userIdentity": {"userName": "", "principalId": "ffff", "accessKeyId": "", "invokedBy": "", "sessionContext": {"attributes": {"creationDate": "", "mfaAuthenticated": "false"}}, "type": "IAMUser", "arn": "", "accountId": ""}, "eventSource": "", "requestID": "123", "userAgent": "", "sourceIPAddress": ""}
""")

print jmespath.search("eventVersion", a)
```

a pre_save is used before the actual database transaction occurs. Since it's an operation that we cannot roll back if the `save()` call fails, I'd be worried about rollback failures. Basically here's my thinking on how I'd imagine a pre_save signal would fail:

 - create an app called `foo`
 - etcd is up, db is down
 - etcd key is created, but db is down so we don't save to the database
 - try again after db is up
 - etcd set fails because key already exists

I'm leaning more towards adding it directly into the `save()` operation, though it looks like we'll have to mock out calls to etcd in order for the tests to pass.

Sorry for the cryptic comments.  The issue is that with the syndication framework, your views will be object instances and not functions.  In particular with Python2, it will be an old-style class due to the way it's written, but I've since realized that's not very relevant.

I had written the `use_view_name` code (naively) assuming that every view would be a function.  But object instances don't have the `__name__` property unless you go through `__class__` first -- oops.

And yes, you should be able to work around this for now by turning off that option.

        x = np.random.normal(size=l)
        start_time = time.time()
        np.nanmean(acf(x, unbiased=True, fft=False)[1:])
        t[i] += (time.time() - start_time)

Hello, I've stumbled upon this with @jjhelmus's help. I noticed this situation happening with R's .libPaths() and then confirmed it also happens with Python's sys.path. (You can trace the backreferences from the conda-forge/python-feedstock above)

I think the argument for root installations potentially needing user site-packages makes sense, however I was wondering if the position had evolved at all over time? It seems like conda is emphasizing reproducible deployments fairly heavily over system administration (thought that is useful as well). 

Given that  [PEP 405](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0405/) specifically calls out how it interacts with PEP 370 in [this section](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0405/#isolation-from-system-site-packages), it does seem like conda should behave similarly. Additionally it looks (from the outside at least) like pip intends to move its default to `--user` (to avoid `sudo pip install`) which will make things appear very strange to beginner users when using conda. 

Imagine for example a user that copy-pastes pip-install instructions from the internet before they really have a chance/need to learn about virtualized environments. I see this pretty consistently in R via `install.packages()` (usually in RStudio), and it's not exactly obvious where and why things break. I would hate to see this happen to beginner Python users as well.

Thanks for reading!

@kalefranz, I'm thinking we need to either remove symbolic linking from the package cache altogether, or change `allow_softlinks` to default to `False`. What happens if the package cache is corrupted or removed? Or a package from one channel has to overwrite a package from another channel? Suddenly the environments that link to it will be corrupted.

That’s interesting. Didn’t know that was their advice. They’re recommendations are based on working around deficiencies in pip, and making sure tensorflow works at all costs, without regard to anything else you’ve got in your environment. And here I mean virtualenv, not a conda environment. Pip just doesn’t have the capability right now to keep environments consistent among all requirements across operations. 

So on one hand I guess I don’t blame them. On the other, they’re really creating difficult-to-diagnose situations for users who are working with anything other than tensorflow.

I put some explanations in the mailing list [here](https://groups.google.com/d/msg/jupyter/jfPC-ROO_XY/sDu3eRbOAgAJ) and [here](https://groups.google.com/d/msg/jupyter/qE6fCyXe6fs/IpxGTDDNAgAJ).

And what @minrk decribes, it complements the info I provided there...

>This hasn't got any less challenging, but if Anaconda feels the need to push environments into the existing kernelspec mechanism, maybe we need to try to work these issues out and produce a more general mechanism.

You raised very well the complexities... now looking into this using the Jupyter dev glasses, I don't know if we want to introduce that complexity and push for a general mechanism... Now as a conda user and CIO dev... I would love to see something naively integrated. How I reconcile my two faces :wink:

In any case, for this specific issue, in nb_conda_kernels we need to deal better with users working with pre-existing ipynb. Pinging @bollwyvl and @stephenakearns.

By the way, one general question. Is there a specific purpose for using parameters in the Verilog/SystemVerilog test-configuration to pass arguments from the command-line ?
In VHDL of course you need generics for this purpose. But Verilog has the $plusargs system task which can be used to pass arguments from the command-line into the code. The advantage of doing this is that $plusargs can be processed without re-elaborating the whole design+TB, whereas parameters/generics are evaluated during elaboration. 
Let me know your thoughts on that.

Hi @BurtBiel @annatisch 

I was curious to investigate this one, so I just try.
In fact, there is a problem around what `top` semantic is. The REST API consider that `top` is the number of elements in each page. I agree that the doc is not explicit on this:
https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/azure/dn790529.aspx

But even with `top=2`, you get the `nextLink` link to get another page of 2 elements and so on.

I think the purpose of `top` is for paging in websites scenario (do not load all pages, load 10 elements and load the others when the user scroll down). One point in that way is that you do not have a `order by` keyword. What is the point to get only 2 random elements? Every limit system comes usually with a "order by" counterpart.

So, doing:
```python
elements = list(rmc.resource_groups.list(top=1))
```
will always get to the entire list, it's just the number of HTTP request under the hood that will change.

If you really want only one random element, you could do that:
```python
elements = rmc.resource_groups.list(top=1).next()
```
which will return only 1 element (i.e. the first page)

I agree that the documentation is not helping here, it's because the gap between the `top` semantic in the REST API and what people expect in Python since @annatisch created an automatic paging class.

With the new change the following will be logged to the console when a None value is passed to a response's header:
``Error handling request

In order to have the actual trace in the error log the following would have to be executed and try/excepted:
```py 
await resp.prepare(request)
```
But:
a) Catching TypeError at such a broad scope (I think) would not be the best practice
b) I would require recalling the exact same command after running `handle_error`. (code repetition)
c) The stack that is returned only includes aiohttp code so (again I think) it wouldn't really help a user of the framework (i.e. developer) understand what he/she did wrong.

Dockerfile

	FROM python:3.5.2-slim

	RUN python3 -m pip install nbgrader
	RUN python3 -m pip install pymongo
	RUN python3 -m pip install nose

	RUN useradd -ms /bin/bash instructor

	RUN nbgrader extension install --symlink --debug
	RUN nbgrader extension activate

	WORKDIR /home/instructor

	RUN mkdir -p /home/instructor/submitted
	RUN mkdir /home/instructor/autograded
        # Maybe we could auto generate and build Dockerfile rather than hardcoding
	RUN mkdir -p /home/instructor/source/assignment_name

	# Folders for mounting...
	RUN mkdir -p /home/instructor/user-submission
	RUN mkdir -p /home/instructor/autograded-docker

	RUN touch .nbgrader.log
	RUN mkdir .jupyter

	ADD autograde.sh /autograde
	RUN chmod 755 /autograde

Will follow-up in the new issue when it’s open, but this is only applied to the very notebook-specific configuration of extensions, not the rest of “normal” traitlets-based configuration that’s shared across packages. You are right that adding .d-style config to that belongs in a higher-level for Jupyter-core or traitlets (see my attempt: https://github.com/ipython/traitlets/issues/242). The fact that extensions use a totally different configuration system is an issue and source of confusion, but not a new one. Adding conf.d to traitlets/core also wouldn’t solve the issue for extensions, because it’s a different setup, so we would have to do it twice anyway. The core.paths are still relevant, this is an additional layer inside a given directory on those paths.

I'd really like to leave this to external tools, and it seems like overrides are the only tricky part. The names and paths of overrides are trivial to query, so I think the right way to support this may be to provide complete and machine-readable output about overrides for external tools to consume.

For example, we could provide a `--json` option that can be used with `peru override list` that prints names and absolute paths of all known overrides as JSON. An external tool could use this to query the paths where overrides live and watch them.

Machine-readable output for query commands may be useful in general (see #165), so we could consider providing a `--json` option for other commands as well to ease automation. The output format is of course debatable. 😃

I'm not sure what this would mean for your modd configuration, since something would have to notice override changes and update the paths that are being watched. It may still be necessary to watch `.peru/overrides` to know when to update the override paths, which is a bit of a hack. This actually makes me question using completely different interfaces for modules and overrides...

Another thought that I want to record here: it is currently difficult to make a callback that uses external data, such as a live image viewer. There are two forms on latency that have to be dealt with:

1. Waiting for the asset registry to 'know' about a new datum_id
2. Waiting for the file to be available on disk

This does nothing to solve (2) but it obviates (1) entirely. A callback will directly receive all the information it needs, in order, to display externally-stored data to the user as soon as that externally-stored data is accessible on disk (which we cannot ever control).

From what I understand the cookie cleaning is somewhat broken in another way too:

* In session scoped browsers cookies is cleared at browser startup (when the cookies is already clean anyways) but since this happens in prepare_browser, it won't be called between tests.

* In function scoped browsers cookie cleanup happens, but it is not very useful since a clean browser is constructed for each test anyways.

My preferred solution would be to just avoid cookie cleaning on function-scoped browsers altogether even if `splinter_clean_cookies_urls` is configured, fix `splinter_clean_cookies_urls` to be called before each test with session scoped browsers and change the default of `splinter_clean_cookies_urls` to `['about:blank']` to preserve the existing behaviour. Users of MS Edge+session scoped browsers just have to set `splinter_clean_cookies_urls` to a real url or `[]` until the issue is fixed in edge/selenium.

(FYI I cannot use session scoped browsers at all since we store a bunch of stuff in localstorage which cannot be reliably cleared via webdriver APIs)

This doesn't seem to be our fault -- the problem seems to be that krb5 doesn't do a `NULL` check before using the `g_OID_equal` macro (see `src/lib/gssapi/mechglue/g_dsp_name_ext.c` lines [97](https://github.com/krb5/krb5/blob/master/src/lib/gssapi/mechglue/g_dsp_name_ext.c#L117) and [117](https://github.com/krb5/krb5/blob/master/src/lib/gssapi/mechglue/g_dsp_name_ext.c#L117)).

A workaround here is tricky -- we shouldn't prevent `display_as` when `Name#name_type` returns `None`, since this *should* work (it will return an error in MIT krb5, but it should be valid).  

Furthermore, testing seems to indicate that for a mechanism name (canonical name), `display_name` (which is used to fetch the name type) will always return `NameType.kerberos_principal`, even if the underlying C `name->name_type` value is `NULL` (see [lib/gssapi/mechglue/g_dsp_name.c:94-103](https://github.com/krb5/krb5/blob/master/src/lib/gssapi/mechglue/g_dsp_name.c#L94-L103) and [lib/gssapi/krb5/disp_name.c:38](https://github.com/krb5/krb5/blob/master/src/lib/gssapi/krb5/disp_name.c#L38)

This is actually a dupe #1370. @cweiske, if you're running Let's Encrypt on your server, I would use `webroot`, otherwise I would use `manual`. More information is available [here](https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/using.html#plugins).

Here you are:

```
cert = /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.org/cert.pem
privkey = /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.org/privkey.pem
chain = /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.org/chain.pem
fullchain = /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.org/fullchain.pem

# Options used in the renewal process
[renewalparams]
authenticator = webroot
installer = None
account = ***************
webroot_path = /var/www/letsencrypt,
[[webroot_map]]
example.org = /var/www/letsencrypt
```

Interesting approach.  This is rather different from what we do in Apache, but it might be workable.  Some questions:

* Do you defer to existing directives, override them, or offer a switch between the two?
* If you defer, there's probably no elegant way for us to update the SSL options we've set, which seems bad.
* Overriding is a better default, though we should think about how to let the user know, and give them an option to cancel (I think we can improve the current Easy / Secure UI step to achieve that).

@beatkind, well I can tell you why the error occurred based on your log. There are files in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal` that contain information about certificates `letsencrypt` previously obtained. On your system, one of these files (which would have been named `www.5space.de.conf` by default), contains a line like the following at the beginning of the file:
```
cert = /etc/letsencrypt/live/www.5space.de/cert.pem
```
This line tells `letsencrypt` that `/etc/letsencypt/live/www.5space.de/cert.pem` is a symlink to the current certificate for a specified set of domains. `letsencrypt` is reading this line and trying to access symlink at the specified path. The problem is this symlink doesn't exist and the client crashes.

The interesting thing is that the `www.5space.de.conf` configuration file is created by `letsencrypt` after the symlink at `/etc/letsencrypt/live/www.5space.de/cert.pem`. Do you have any idea how `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal/www.5space.de.conf` could have been modified or the symlink at `/etc/letsencrypt/live/www.5space.de/cert.pem` deleted? If not, can you describe to me what you have done `letsencrypt` so far?

if I do hard delete message,only I can receive {pres} message,the other do not receive {pre}.Is this nomal?

> 1.If I do hard delete message,only I can receive {pres} message,the other do not receive {pres}.Is this nomal?

If that happens when you indeed hard delete a message then it's a bug, but I don't think you are hard-deleting a message. What most likely is happening is that you are requesting to hard-delete a message but don't have a `D` permission. The server silently converts your hard-delete to soft-delete and notifies just you that the message was deleted for you.

>2.If I want to delete messages for everybody,what need I do?

Send a `{del hard=true}` and have a `D` permission.

> 3. When I set {sub.mode} to "JRWPAD",I get "JRWPA" every time.why?
 
In the first case you are trying to (a) set default permissions on a P2P topic and separately (b) set a GIVEN permission for the other user. For (a) you get the first 403 "permission denied" because the operation makes no sense. For (b) you successfully change the GIVEN permission of the other user (200 OK) then maybe confusingly the server reports back your own permissions instead of the changed permission of the other user. 

In the second case you just do (b) alone and you get the same response as described above. 

Currently there is no way for a user to gain a `D` permission in a P2P topic:
https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/master/server/topic.go#L853
It's a product decision. It seems to be OK to allow a user to hard-delete his own messages (and maybe it should be added as a configurable option) but certainly not OK to allow one user to hard-delete any message in a P2P conversation.

This is more one of those principle of least surprise things.  

When I first started testing the library, one of the first things I tried was `tzwin('Eastern Daylight Time')` since it was daylight savings time.  I got the above error.  I figured no problem, it doesn't expect 'Eastern Daylight Time' as a valid timezone.  So I tried variants like 'US Eastern Daylight Time', and 'EDT' and all lowercase since it was quicker than looking up the docs.  

Those failed so, of course the next thing I tried was `tzwinlocal().tzname(datetime.datetime.now())` to find a valid `tzname()` and was suprised to see it then respond with `Eastern Daylight Time`.  

It would be natural to assume that any name returned should be able to be used as an argument.  In addition, while I have yet to do any tests with pickling dateutil objects, I was wondering if this would present any issues there.

@Dveim Not a general workaround, but here's what I've been using to hack around it for the purposes of using `joblib.Memory` within dask multiprocessing. IIRC the `func_name` vs. `__name__` hack only works because of the way `joblib.Memory.cache` looks for its cached function's name, but maybe this can spark some ideas for you.

```py
def wraps_workaround(f):
    """
    Workaround a cloudpickle bug that causes joblib.Memory to lose the cached func __name__ inside dask processes
    - https://github.com/cloudpipe/cloudpickle/issues/177

    This workaround can be deleted when these asserts pass:

        import dask
        from joblib.func_inspect import *

        load = Load()

        # Works regardless of bug, since threads don't invoke cloudpickle
        assert dask.delayed(format_signature)(load._metadata).compute(get=dask_get_for_scheduler_name('threads')) == \
            ('load._metadata', '_metadata()')

        # Requires bugfix to work, since processes marshal using cloudpickle
        assert dask.delayed(format_signature)(load._metadata).compute(get=dask_get_for_scheduler_name('processes')) == \
            ('load._metadata', '_metadata()')

    """
    def decorator(g):
        g = wraps(f)(g)
        g.func_name = f.__name__
        return g
    return decorator
```

I try to detect and handle as many problematic cases as I can, but every now and then something would slip through the cracks. Regular expressions in general are complicated, so my syntax checking does a quick look to detect any obvious things that could go wrong, but it wouldn't raise an error on these sorts of triggers:

```
+ )invalid( parenthesis
+ (or <this) thing>
```

...because the brackets are all balanced, but they'd result in invalid regular expressions. I don't know of any better ("better" defined as being concise, non-messy source code) to validate a regexp apart from actually attempting to use it as a regexp. Python has slow regular expressions in general and RiveScript aggressively caches as many as it can up front (after parse time), so testing every trigger regexp could add some latency during parsing to try each trigger it sees. There's also a [regexp that tests regexps](https://stackoverflow.com/a/172316/3768440), but that's getting into crazy territory. :wink:

The safest way for an end user to ensure that an uncaught exception in RiveScript doesn't crash your whole program is to put calls to RiveScript in a try/except block.

```python
try:
    reply = rs.reply(username, message)
except Exception as e:
    log.exception("Unexpected exception getting a reply: {}".format(e))
    reply = "An internal error has occurred and has been reported to my botmaster."
```

As to the z3 timeout, z3 is the constraint solver we use. In some cases its super hard/undecidable to say if something is satisfiable, in which case we don't want the solver to keep working forever. This isn't a super weird situation, and the timeout just tells the solver how extensively to look for a certain situation. In the end we need to make a tradeoff between how much possibilities we can explore (smt solving, and symbolic execution) vs resource costs. We are planning on providing customization options through the cli, to allow the user to choose these parameters (fitting them to their resources and requirements).  I'll add a logging rule (that logs timeouts) which will make it easier to find what values work best

Hi @christinedekock,

Recently, I also explored similar situation as yours, where in addition to positive implicit feedback data, I also have implicit negative feedback. The way I approach the problem is instead of sampling negative items at random, I modified a bit the sampling strategy to sample from implicit negative feedback. This is somewhat intuitive IMO, because if we already have the negative feedback for each users, why would we choose at random? This is even more relevant if we have sufficient number of negative feedbacks. 

Comparing to random sampling, this small tweak lead to better MAP with fewer epoch. I think it helps the model to converge faster (in terms of epoch). Based on further analysis I did, I would like to answer the above question. The answer is that **it doesn't matter**.
A caching library does not need to try to keep becoming smarter (it is not competing to be better than the database). I would like to answer some observations from further analysis done by me.

- I analysed a lot of queries done from my DB, and the problem always is a lot of SQL queries. I analyzed those queries using the helpful django-debug-toolbar and silk
I was able to reduce time for executing SQL queries using prefetching. (I brought down 2000 SQL queries to 38)

- Django caching API allows to take care of simple business cases. I had a use-case of reading nearly all data from DB. Since this was aggregate data and consistency doesn't matter, I simply cached each object(including relationships) from the DB manually. (and obtained qs from DB still) 
This simply worked, and invalidation are done based on default settings.

Why the optimizations were unnecessary
--
If you don't have a lot of time to spend on the above ideas, there's a way which is 1000 times simpler. Simply adding cachalot to my application allowed me to get enough gains that I wouldn't have needed to do any further deep-dives above.

**Why does Cachalot work in the case above?**
Because that's how we usually code with the ORM. We ask for relationships when we want them, and not doing pre-fetches/optimizations helps us to write code which is easily cachable.

I will give a real world example here from the LeanAgri setup:

```python
class Crop(Model)
    
class Fertilizer(Model)

class Schedule(Model)
    crop = ForeignKey(Crop)
    fertilizer = ManyToManyField(Fertilizer)

for schedule in Schedule.objects.all():
    print schedule.crop.get_data()
    for fertilizer in schedule.fertilizer.all():
          print fertilizer.get_data()
```

Why was it written this way? Because that's how a lot of code was written to begin with. There were no thoughts of optimizations, there were business use-cases which keep on getting added over time. 
**Cachalot will infact perform better** without these optimizations added since multiple objects will share the same queries and thus be able to take advantage of those cachings.

However, not optimising our code to use pre-fetches limits our un-cached queries and make them a lot slower. So it is a good idea to still optimize and pre-fetch. 

**TLDR**: It doesn't matter what you "think" would your use case be and whether it would be handled well by the caching setup. To put it simply, it will be. Unless you dig deep into what is actually happening in your application, the no-brainer way of putting cachalot in your application is going to make things considerably better.

@wintersteiger 

> This looks a lot better now, this is mergable. I still expect build and test failures in the next few builds, mainly due to the changes in the API generation code. See also notes below.

Thanks for merging :)

> Can you tell me what combinations of systems, APIs, and Python versions this has been tested already?

The CMake build system was tested on

* Arch Linux with python2 and 3 with clang and gcc using CMake 3.4.3
* Ubuntu 14.04 with python2, gcc and CMake 2.8.12.2

The python build system was tested on

* Arch Linux with python3 and gcc

> Some notes:
>
> Additional, new .py files unnecessary, could all have stayed in mk_util.py. Perhaps move those new .py files to contrib/cmake as that's the only consumer at this time. Also, lots of unnecessary code duplication in them.

Those extra scripts are necessary but that isn't obvious unless you look at how the CMake build system invokes them. The extra python scripts I've added are used as executable programs during the build (the CMake build system generates the files at build time, not configure time). To me this was the only sane way to generate the necessary files to build Z3 from CMake because the logic for generating those files is **very** complicated and reimplementing that logic in CMake seemed like a waste of time when instead I could refactor the existing python code so I could call it from a python script by passing the correct command line parameters.

By code duplication I assume you mean the ``def main(args):...`` stuff? That really is necessary because each script is a standalone program that can be invoked on the command line. Each script is slightly different and it wasn't obvious at the time if I could refactor this because I was learning what files needed be generated as I went along.

> (some parts of) all APIs now seem to be built/included when some output-directory is set instead of is_*_enabled?! Unnecessary and unintuitive.

Which part of the code are you talking about? Do you mean the ``update_api.generate_files()`` method? If so you there is no ``_*_enabled()`` usage there because those functions are part of the python build system and I was trying to avoid as much coupling to it as possible. Unfortunately this isn't entirely consistent as there are uses of ``is_verbose()`` lying around but I didn't want to change too much in one go. If I had used the ``_*_enabled()`` functions I would have to start modifying global variables from my scripts which (IMHO) is horrible and should be avoided where possible.

I would say "unintuitive" here is very subjective. What I was trying to do here was have the ability to generate whatever API files I needed. The old code didn't really give you a choice, it always writes the ``.cpp``, ``.h`` and ``z3_core.py`` files. At the same time I also needed to be able to choose where those files are written. The existing python build system just emits those file into the source tree but that is generally considered bad practice so I needed to control the directory those files would be created in. Therefore it seemed logical to have the ``generate_files()`` function to take arguments (rather than rely on global state) and have those arguments be the output directories for particular API bindings. If the directory is set to python's ``None`` then that would indicate that emission of those binding files is not desired.

The function could probably do with some comments though explaining this. Would you like me to add some?

Aside: The reason that writing generated files into the source tree (rather than the build directory) is considered bad practice is because it potentially prevents having multiple build directories that all share the same source directory.

> Big chunks of code update_api.py have apparently been moved with slight nor no modification, such that no diff tool will show whether there are no, minor, or major changes in behaviour. As a consequence we'll have to test all APIs on all systems to see whether something broke.

This is unfortunate but necessary. It couldn't make progress with ``update_api.py`` without refactoring to be more like a callable module rather than what it was (a massive script).

> Contrary to file name and description, mk_consts_files.py seems to generate only consts.py instead of all consts/enums files for all APIs. Unclear whether this is an additional, Python-only "feature", or whether the Python API was the only "interesting" one so far.

My intention is to add support for the other bindings at a later date when I (or someone else) has time to add support for them. I did the python bindings first because

* The python bindings are the easiest to build/install (i.e. nothing to compile)
* The Python bindings are also very popular so supporting them first seemed like a priority.

One problem is that the thresholds can be hard to get right or be too sensitive. Say you remove lines of code and the associated tests, your overall coverage can go down even though you haven't introduced any uncovered lines.

There is the Con that @dorukozturk pointed out where you now cannot add new lines of code without also covering them, which can be an impediment to development speed. Generally that is a good thing, to slow us down, but if you add boilerplate lines of code that don't need to be covered they can still trip the thresholds.

We could spend some time to tune the thresholds, but when someone is pushing hard for a deadline these can failing tests could be annoying.

Generally I think this is a good thing because it is a guard rail to encourage us to do the right thing. It can be overridden by admin authority (i.e. a failing test that can be forced to be merged), but at least it adds visibility.

nteract responds to the same payloads that come from `_repr_html_`. One thing we started adopting a few years ago (and jupyterlab eventually brought in) was custom payloads, particularly where there is a specific schema between a JS library and what the python side would send. We do this for vega, plotly, and others by allowing them to send their own data type + mimetype. For Plotly, they wrote a [versioned schema](https://help.plot.ly/json-chart-schema/) that we happily [send on to their JS library](https://github.com/nteract/nteract/blob/master/packages/transform-plotly/src/index.js#L39). In order to do this though, we bake in access to these visualization libraries as part of our shipped app. So long as pyecharts has their own schema that would be adhered to as well as maintenance work on supporting the React component that wraps it, then we can incorporate it. We don't have a dynamic way for users to add on new libraries in nteract yet.

angr is, at its core, an emulator. Hooking is instrumentation for that emulator! To run the emulator as a symbolic execution engine, you should create an emulated state, then put that state in a path or a path group, and then start stepping that state forward. All of this is described in the documentation.

Hello! In general, claripy is very well-insulated from the end user - you should never have to use it except to create ASTs. Instead, there is a proxy for that function on SimState: `state.satisfiable()`. Given a path, the corresponding state at the end of that path is `path.state`. (This will change soon: we are working to remove Path entirely and replace it with SimState)

However, assuming you are using a recent version of angr, all paths that you see through path.step() or pathgroup.active or pathgroup.found should always be satisfiable, as we discard unsatisfiable paths. In older versions, an option called LAZY_SOLVES was enabled by default, which allowed unsatisfiable paths to stick around for as long as possible. If you are seeing unsat results when trying to solve, you should upgrade angr.

If you're using the `path_group.explore` method, you can pass in a function for the `avoid` kwarg. That function will be run on each path, with the path as the argument, to filter paths at every step. Currently, you'll have to craft a function to check the backward slice and decide whether to filter the path.

@ltfish, in the future, it'd be cool to add a BackwardSlice.filter_path function that could just be directly passed in as the `avoid` parameter to `path_group.explore`!

Hi Raja,

I suspect this is a misunderstanding between threads in Python and the 
pthreads in the underlying system.

When you start python, it runs as a single process with a single 
pthread. This is due to design choices in Python that makes it single 
threaded by nature as seen from the operating system. Thus everything 
will run on the same core.

The only way around this in Python is to spawn off new Python processes 
where you then communicate through pipes or something similar. This is 
done for the communication with Dirac (one for the interactive commands, 
one for the monitoring) and that is probably why you see three cores active.

The NumWorkerThreads controls the number of Python threads. This is 
useful to increase if there is a limit due to waiting for authentication 
from a server etc, as it can then use the local core more efficiently.

Cheers,
         Ulrik.

This is very important:

1. Unit tests should only be testing one thing at a time. **Maybe** two if the two things are very similar... but they should be testing a **unit*. This is so that when a unit test fails, we know exactly what is failing. If the unit test is testing 4 things at once and it fails, which of the 4 things failed? I need to go debug to know that and that is wasted time.
2. Every class is in a different file. A class is some code that is isolated of its dependencies. So, if you are _unit testing_ a class, you should be testing it isolated of its dependencies, so that even if dependencies fail, your unit tests do not get affected. Unit tests are grouped into different files because every test file unit tests just one class. If you are importing dependencies, [like this](https://github.com/jupyter-incubator/sparkmagic/pull/198/files#diff-498e0d5682020c4fc81dfb940efe63feR8), it's a pretty good indication of a [code smell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Code_smell).

So, please break your unit tests into **unit** tests and put them in the files they belong at.

I'll chime in here, too: I'm currently setting up an environment similar to HuddleLamp ([Website](http://huddlelamp.org/), [Paper](http://dl.acm.org/citation.cfm?id=2669500), [Video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XkmwG588zp0)) with the F200, and the distance from camera to surface is ~ 80 cm. For this setup to work reliably, I've had to slightly increase the Motion/Range tradeoff.

I don't own an SR300 so I don't really know which parameters you can tune, but I think you should try 2 simple things that might solve your problem (under conditions), in order : 

- If your object-to-be-scanned if located at a fixed distance from the camera. -> Measure that distance precisely and set the value in the Intel RealSense Viewer software under _Advanced Controls/Depth Table/Depth Clamp Min_. What it's going to do is restricting the depth of the scanned area by ignoring anything **below** this distance. I understood that you want to see something behind a glass, and not something in front of it, correct ? By setting the correct distance, you could ignore anything, including the glass, "too close" to the camera, which is quite nice. The downside is that you are stuck with that distance and can't move your model/camera closer along the Z axis anymore. You'll get better results with the camera and the glass being parallel.
Be also aware that the units of the distance input is directly correlated with the value set under _Controls/Depth Units_ (ie Depth Units : 0.001 -> Depth Clamp : [mm])

- As I see, the artifact is pretty small. It's not that bad, so it should be easy to correct with some corrections, as you said. -> Go in the Intel RealSense Viewer software, and tick the _Post-Processing_ box. In here, some options will impact the filling of the holes : 
1) If you don't really care about the amount of vertices of your output, increase the _Decimation Filter_. From what I understood, the _Filter Magnitude_ will divide the number of vertices by 2^(input value) (ie Filter Magnitude : 3 -> Output number of points = Max number of vertices/2^3). So basically, """less points = less artifact""" :P
2) Under _Post-Processing/Spacial Filter/Holes filling_, switch to _2, 4 or 8 pixels radius_ depending of the size of your holes. I wouldn't recommend to increase this value too much because it'll deteriorate your output, specially the border of your object.
3) Finally, under the _Post-Processing_ menu again, there is the _Hole Filling Filter_. I find this one to be quite powerfull (a bit too much imho), but really difficult to use, so tune it with care.

Hi @bitzombie1 ,

The python API is almost 1-1 as the C++ API. Just like the `rs-sensor-control` example, you can use python API to get/set options via a sensor instance.
The `pipeline` encapsulates the device (and its sensors) so there is not API for controlling options via the `pipeline` itself. 
The way to do that is using the `pipeline_profile` from which you can `get_device()` and then you are back to the device and sensor APIs.
The `pipeline_profile` is the return value of `pipeline.start()` or `config.resolve()`.
The `align-depth2color.py` example show something similar to that...

Here is a snippet for getting what you want:
```py
import pyrealsense2 as rs
pipeline = rs.pipeline()
config = rs.config()
profile = config.resolve(pipeline) # does not start streaming
profile = pipeline.start(config) # Start streaming
depth_sensor = profile.get_device().first_depth_sensor()
exposure = depth_sensor.get_option(rs.option.exposure)
exposure = depth_sensor.set_option(rs.option.exposure, 0)
```

When PyPI is the package index, `Python-Requires` metadata is available even for sdists (my initial guess about this was thankfully incorrect), and pip knows to skip the `futures` 3.2.0 sdist:

```
The package https://pypi.python.org/packages/1f/9e/7b2ff7e965fc654592269f2906ade1c7d705f1bf25b7d469fa153f7d19eb/futures-3.2.0.tar.gz#md5=d1b299a06b96ccb59f70324716dc0016 (from https://pypi.python.org/simple/futures/) (requires-python:>=2.6, <3) is incompatible with the pythonversion in use. Re dependencies:

> what's the cleanest way to define a transmission that depends on libraries the caller may or may not have? The "import and catch ImportError" approach feels hacky, but I also do not want to make tornado a hard dependency for libhoney.

That's what I did for the Ruby library instrumentations. I wanted installing the beeline gem to also install all the instrumentations (activerecord-honeycomb, faraday-honeycomb, etc), but *not* to install the libraries they instrument, so that your app doesn't end up depending on activerecord if it doesn't use it. e.g. activerecord-honeycomb does not declare a dependency on the activerecord gem, but instead [checks for it at runtime](https://github.com/honeycombio/activerecord-honeycomb/blob/572640dc376da9fb8670c33a48b194681c69482a/lib/activerecord-honeycomb.rb) and emits a warning if it is missing.

(The beeline gem also does some dependency detection to decide which instrumentations to activate, in order to avoid emitting a bunch of warnings for libraries you already knew your app wasn't using.)

@TobiasReichert, thanks for raising this issue!

This seems to be an issue with the Advanced Software Framework (ASF) itself. In particular, both `asf/sam0/utils/compiler.h` and `arm-none-eabi/include/sys/cdefs.h` (included by the former) define `__always_inline`.

One hack is to apply the following patch to `xdk-asf-3.40.0/sam0/utils/compiler.h`:
```
132c132
< #  define __always_inline             __attribute__((__always_inline__))
---
> //#  define __always_inline             __attribute__((__always_inline__))
```
Basically, avoid defining `__always_inline` since gcc-arm does that for you already.

Add `get_cdp_neighbors` and `get_cdp_neighbors_details` methods .

The data format for `get_cdp_neighbors` is the same as `get_lldp_neighbors`. But `get_cdp_neighbors_details` has to be a different format as CDP does not return the same information as LLDP does.

Having the explicit methods allows the user to choose which method to use based on his knowledge of the network, or try both.

The `get_layer2_neighbors` and `get_layer2_neighbors_details` should return the combination of both commands. This is necessary to cover a scenario where a device has both LLDP and CDP enabled and have CDP neighbors in some ports and LLDP neighbors in others.

Let me share my summary, and I welcome your comment for anything wrong:
1. Train a model specifically for classification.
 ex) `fasttext supervised -input your_training_text -output your_model`
2. Get sentence level vector with the model.
 ex) `fasttext print-sentence-vectors your_model.bin < your_test_sentence.txt`

Depending on the task you want to perform, you may simply average word embeddings of all words in the paragraph.

According to [Kenter et al. 2016](http://aclweb.org/anthology/P/P16/P16-1089.pdf), this approach "has proven to be a strong baseline or feature across a multitude of tasks", such as short text similarity tasks.

However, according to [Le and Mikolov](https://cs.stanford.edu/~quocle/paragraph_vector.pdf), this method performs poorly for sentiment analysis tasks and/or long texts, because it "loses the word order in the same way as the standard bag-of-words models do" and "fail[s] to recognize many sophisticated linguistic phenomena, for instance sarcasm".

Apart from that, the actual problem is the following, I would guess: pybind want to initialize the new class as `new Cpp<Class>{std::forward<Args>(args)...}` which would be `new std::binary_function<double, double, bool>{ other }` where `other` has type `const std::binary_function<double, double, bool> &`. Since it is a POD (an empty struct, no specific constructor, ...), the brace initialization wants to member-wise initialize the struct, but you're passing 1 argument while the struct only has 0 members. Hence the error `excess elements in struct initializer`.

The workaround is to `py::init([](const std::binary_function<double, double, bool> & other) { return new std::binary_function<double, double, bool>(other); })` (where you can choose to use `()` instead of `{}`). 

See also #1070. So maybe it would be an idea for pybind11 to provide some kind of a tag to `init` that could switch between brace-initialization and parentheses-calls?

Very interesting. Based on these backtraces, it seems like you've stumbled upon a super rare where a newly allocated type object is visited by the GC, which gets triggered by some string routines. Since it is not fully initialized, bad things ensue.

That line in the documentation (and your code) really should be written as `py::array_t<double> result(buf1.size);`.  As it's written now, it's invoking implicit conversion constructors, but apparently is picking the wrong ones.

This is a deeper issue in pybind11, though: we really should be marking almost all of the single-argument constructors (or multi-argument with defaults for 2nd and beyond arguments) as `explicit` (as recommended in the [https://github.com/isocpp/CppCoreGuidelines/blob/master/CppCoreGuidelines.md#Rc-explicit](C++ Core Guidelines)): otherwise we're defining implicit conversions in all sorts of places (like this) that we don't really intend it.

Right now, we're saying:
```C++
py::array_t<double> foo = 13;
```
and
```C++
void foo(py::array_t<double> f) { ... }
foo(13);
```
are valid, and I really don't think they should be.  This goes far beyond `array_t`, however: we have unintended implicit conversion in many other places, as well.

(As a side note, this particular C++ feature has always irritated me: implicit conversion really should be an opt-in rather than opt-out feature; that ship sailed long, long ago, however, so we're stuck with it).

Primarily due to the usefulness of `call_guard`, a `scoped_estream_redirect` has been added with `cerr` defaults. This allows both `cout` and `cerr` to be redirected easily:

      py::call_guard<py::scoped_ostream_redirect,
                     py::scoped_estream_redirect>());
```
